However, it conflicts with the operational need for early detection, making it a challenging trade-off to evaluate without understanding the domain context of dropout intervention timing.

hypothesis: A transformer-based ranker that uses only product title and query text will outperform a learning-to-rank model augmented with click-through and purchase co-occurrence features for marketplace search relevance, measured by next-day nDCG on held-out searches, because the semantic encoder better handles sparse long-tail queries and new catalog items.
domain: E-commerce marketplace search and product discovery for a large consumer retail platform with frequent catalog turnover
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer Ranker vs. Learning-to-Rank with Popularity Features

### 1. Hypothesis Restatement
We hypothesize that a transformer-based dual-encoder ranker, trained only on query text and product titles, will outperform a learning-to-rank baseline that includes click-through and purchase co-occurrence features. This advantage should emerge because the transformer learns semantic relevance rather than position-biased popularity signals, and thus generalizes better to sparse long-tail queries and newly listed products.

### 2. Data Collection and Label Construction
We would collect 18 months of search logs from the production marketplace platform, including all query text, product impressions (title, brand, category, and other attributes), clicks, add-to-cart events, purchases, and precise timestamps. Simultaneously, we would commission ~500–1000 editorial relevance judgments from independent human annotators (3–5 judges per query-product pair) on a diverse, representative sample of search sessions. These judgments serve as unbiased ground truth and are collected independently of any model to avoid circularity.

Known data quality issue: clicks and purchases are position-biased (users see top-ranked results first and are more likely to click them regardless of true relevance). We address this by estimating propensity scores from training data using inverse propensity scoring (IPS) or doubly robust methods, and applying these weights consistently during training and evaluation. Propensity models are fit on training data only and frozen for validation and test evaluation.

### 3. Train/Validation/Test Split Strategy
We use a **query-item stratified split with item-cold-start controls**:

- **Query-item stratification:** Search impressions are partitioned into train, validation, and held-out test subsets in proportions corresponding to the planned development workflow, while preserving the distribution of query frequency bands, relevance labels, and other major slice variables across all subsets. This ensures each subset contains comparable mixtures of head and tail queries, preventing model selection from being driven by one unusually easy or difficult demand segment.
  
- **Item cold-start stratification:** Within the partitioning process, we identify products whose first observed appearance falls within 30 days of an impression and label them as "cold-start." Validation and test subsets are constrained to include ~30% cold-start items and ~70% warm items, matching realistic catalog churn. This split directly isolates the hypothesis claim: if the transformer only outperforms on warm items, it is learning popularity, not semantics.

**Why this approach:** Marketplace search data has strong class imbalance across query frequency strata and substantial catalog turnover. Preserving the same query-frequency and item-age composition across development and evaluation subsets makes the comparison between the transformer and the baseline sharper, because any measured difference is less likely to come from one split containing a systematically different mix of tail queries or newly listed products.

### 4. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing
**Transformer ranker features:** Query text and product title, tokenized and embedded via a shared vocabulary fit on training queries and titles. No manual feature engineering.

**LTR baseline features:** Query text and product title (same as transformer) plus four engineered features: (1) click frequency (number of clicks on product given query in training window), (2) purchase co-occurrence (Jaccard similarity between users who clicked this query and users who purchased this product), (3) add-to-cart rate, and (4) category match (TF-IDF similarity or hand-coded signals). All four features are computed from training data only, ensuring the baseline is not advantaged by data leakage.

**Preprocessing fit point:** All transformers (tokenizers, vocabulary, propensity score estimators) are fit on the training set only. Validation and test sets use the fitted tokenizer and propensity model without refitting. This prevents validation/test statistics from influencing preprocessing.

### 5. Model Architecture and Fair Baseline
**Transformer model:** Dual-encoder architecture (separate or shared transformer layers for query and product title) trained end-to-end with contrastive loss (InfoNCE or triplet loss) on query-product pairs, then fine-tuned on propensity-weighted ranking loss (e.g., LambdaMART-style gradient on weighted engagement signals). Architecture: 12-layer transformer or BERT-mini equivalent, ~110M parameters or smaller depending on computational budget.

**Baseline model:** Learning-to-rank model (LambdaMART or gradient-boosted ranker via XGBoost/LightGBM in ranking mode) with identical inputs (query and title) and the four engineered features. The baseline receives equal hyperparameter tuning effort: grid search over learning rate, tree depth, iterations, and regularization on the validation set.

**Why this baseline is fair:** Both models see the same core text signals (query and title). The baseline's additional features are designed to capture exactly what the hypothesis claims the transformer should outperform: popularity signals (clicks, co-occurrence). If the transformer wins, it is because it learns semantics better, not because it has more or better features.

### 6. Hyperparameter Tuning and Model Selection
Both models are tuned on the validation set using inverse-propensity-weighted AUROC as the selection metric. Grid search is performed over reasonable hyperparameter ranges (learning rate, regularization strength, model size). The test set is held out and never touched during tuning. Once both models are finalized on validation performance, they are each evaluated exactly once on the test set.

### 7. Evaluation Metrics and Primary/Secondary Measures
**Primary metric:** **Inverse-propensity-weighted AUROC** on the test set. AUROC is computed from editorial judgments (where available) or propensity-weighted engagement labels (where not), and is reported overall and **separately for cold-start vs. warm items**. Justification: the metric captures how reliably each model separates relevant from non-relevant products across both common and rare queries, which is central to the claim that semantic representations should generalize beyond popularity signals.

**Secondary metrics:**
- nDCG@10 (quality of the ranked top results)
- nDCG@5 (shorter user attention span)
- Recall@10 (useful items in top 10?)
- MRR (mean reciprocal rank of first relevant item)
- **Cold-start item nDCG (stratified):** If transformer excels here and LTR baseline struggles, the hypothesis is supported. If both perform equally on cold-start, the transformer's advantage on warm items may reflect learned popularity.
- **Rare query nDCG:** Performance on queries appearing <5 times in training. This tests the long-tail generalization claim.
- **Kendall's tau correlation against editorial judgments:** On the held-out editorial evaluation set, we measure rank correlation to unbiased ground truth.

### 8. Confound Control and Scope
**Popularity confound:** Cold-start stratification isolates this directly. We report AUROC overall and nDCG separately for cold and warm items to determine whether any advantage is concentrated in newer products or reflects a broader relevance signal.

**Seasonality confound:** Query-frequency and cold-start stratification ensure that development and evaluation subsets contain comparable demand composition rather than being dominated by a single traffic regime.

**Position bias:** Propensity-weighted loss and metrics handle this; propensity models are fit on training data only.

**Query frequency:** Rare query performance is explicitly measured and reported.

### 9. What This Experiment Would and Would Not Establish
**Would establish:** Whether the transformer ranker outperforms LTR on held-out relevance discrimination in this marketplace, and whether the advantage holds on cold-start items and rare queries. If so, it supports the hypothesis that semantic encoding helps with long-tail and novel items.

**Would NOT establish:** Causality in a mechanistic sense (e.g., "the transformer learns semantics"). We observe correlation (better separation of relevant from non-relevant items, especially on cold-start items) but cannot directly prove the transformer is not learning some other signal (e.g., title length, brand affinity). However, the cold-start and rare-query stratification strongly constrain alternative explanations.

**Scope:** Results apply to this marketplace platform and the 18-month data window. Generalization to other platforms or different query distributions is out of scope.

planted_issues.description: The use of a query-item stratified split across the full 18-month horizon allows future user interactions and evolving popularity signals to contaminate training examples intended for earlier evaluation periods. This violates temporal order and introduces leakage: a product's popularity in month 18 could influence its representation in a training example from month 6 that is used to evaluate performance in month 3, invalidating the generalization claim to unseen catalog items and new queries. The hypothesis hinges on robustness to catalog turnover and long-tail queries, making this a critical confound.

Using AUROC as the primary metric instead of nDCG misaligns the evaluation with the business objective and the stated hypothesis. AUROC measures the overall ability to separate relevant from non-relevant items across all positions, but the operational context of marketplace search prioritizes ranking quality in the top few positions where users actually engage. A model could improve AUROC by better separating low-ranked irrelevant items without improving user experience. Since the hypothesis claims superiority in real-world search relevance, which is navigationally biased toward top results, the lack of a position-aware primary metric undermines the experiment's validity.

must_not_claim.claim: The absence of temporal split is a flaw because it doesn't account for seasonality.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: This design did include cold-start stratification and query-frequency controls, which the corrupted narrative falsely presents as sufficient to address temporal confounds. However, a true temporal split is necessary in this domain to prevent popularity signal leakage, so this is a red herring: the real flaw is the lack of chronological partitioning, not the mitigation strategy.

must_not_claim.claim: Using nDCG as a secondary metric while using AUROC primary introduces inconsistency.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Using multiple metrics across depths is valid and common practice. The issue is not duplication, but that AUROC is inappropriate as the *primary* metric for a ranking task where top-of-list quality matters most; nDCG@10 would have been the correct primary choice aligned with user behavior and business goals.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic correctly identifies that the query-item stratified split introduces temporal leakage by mixing time-ordered examples, undermining generalization to future catalog items. The defender cannot plausibly argue that cross-sectional stratification suffices in this domain.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic explains that splitting by query-item without respecting time allows future engagement data to influence model training for earlier periods, which invalidates the cold-start claim because item novelty becomes confounded with temporal popularity.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic points out that AUROC fails to capture the business-relevant quality of placing relevant items at the top of the list, and that this misalignment undermines the hypothesis about real-world search relevance. The defender cannot justify AUROC as more appropriate than nDCG for a top-rank-focused use case.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic argues that the hypothesis emphasizes performance in real search traffic where position matters, and that AUROC treats early and late misrankings equally, potentially rewarding improvements irrelevant to user experience.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Transformer Ranker vs. Learning-to-Rank with Popularity Features

### 1. Hypothesis Restatement
We hypothesize that a transformer-based dual-encoder ranker, trained only on query text and product titles, will outperform a learning-to-rank baseline that includes click-through and purchase co-occurrence features. This advantage should emerge because the transformer learns semantic relevance rather than position-biased popularity signals, and thus generalizes better to sparse long-tail queries and newly listed products.

### 2. Data Collection and Label Construction
We would collect 18 months of search logs from the production marketplace platform, including all query text, product impressions (title, brand, category, and other attributes), clicks, add-to-cart events, purchases, and precise timestamps. Simultaneously, we would commission ~500–1000 editorial relevance judgments from independent human annotators (3–5 judges per query-product pair) on a diverse, representative sample of search sessions. These judgments serve as unbiased ground truth and are collected independently of any model to avoid circularity.

Known data quality issue: clicks and purchases are position-biased (users see top-ranked results first and are more likely to click them regardless of true relevance). We address this by estimating propensity scores from training data using inverse propensity scoring (IPS) or doubly robust methods, and applying these weights consistently during training and evaluation. Propensity models are fit on training data only and frozen for validation and test evaluation.

### 3. Train/Validation/Test Split Strategy
We use a **temporal + item-cold-start stratified split**:

- **Temporal:** Months 1–12 for training; months 13–15 for validation; months 16–18 for held-out test. This prevents future information (promotions, inventory changes, seasonality) from leaking into the training window and ensures the test set reflects real-world catalog turnover.
  
- **Item cold-start stratification:** Within each split window, we identify products appearing for the first time ≤30 days before the window begins and label them as "cold-start." We stratify validation and test sets to include ~30% cold-start items and ~70% warm items, matching realistic catalog churn. This split directly isolates the hypothesis claim: if the transformer only outperforms on warm items, it is learning popularity, not semantics.

**Why this approach:** Marketplace search data exhibits severe temporal and popularity confounds. A random split would allow the model to learn historical item popularity and seasonal query patterns, masking whether the transformer actually generalizes to novel items and queries. The temporal split prevents this; the cold-start stratification tests the core claim.

### 4. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing
**Transformer ranker features:** Query text and product title, tokenized and embedded via a shared vocabulary fit on training queries and titles. No manual feature engineering.

**LTR baseline features:** Query text and product title (same as transformer) plus four engineered features: (1) click frequency (number of clicks on product given query in training window), (2) purchase co-occurrence (Jaccard similarity between users who clicked this query and users who purchased this product), (3) add-to-cart rate, and (4) category match (TF-IDF similarity or hand-coded signals). All four features are computed from training data only, ensuring the baseline is not advantaged by data leakage.

**Preprocessing fit point:** All transformers (tokenizers, vocabulary, propensity score estimators) are fit on the training set only. Validation and test sets use the fitted tokenizer and propensity model without refitting. This prevents validation/test statistics from influencing preprocessing.

### 5. Model Architecture and Fair Baseline
**Transformer model:** Dual-encoder architecture (separate or shared transformer layers for query and product title) trained end-to-end with contrastive loss (InfoNCE or triplet loss) on query-product pairs, then fine-tuned on propensity-weighted ranking loss (e.g., LambdaMART-style gradient on weighted engagement signals). Architecture: 12-layer transformer or BERT-mini equivalent, ~110M parameters or smaller depending on computational budget.

**Baseline model:** Learning-to-rank model (LambdaMART or gradient-boosted ranker via XGBoost/LightGBM in ranking mode) with identical inputs (query and title) and the four engineered features. The baseline receives equal hyperparameter tuning effort: grid search over learning rate, tree depth, iterations, and regularization on the validation set.

**Why this baseline is fair:** Both models see the same core text signals (query and title). The baseline's additional features are designed to capture exactly what the hypothesis claims the transformer should outperform: popularity signals (clicks, co-occurrence). If the transformer wins, it is because it learns semantics better, not because it has more or better features.

### 6. Hyperparameter Tuning and Model Selection
Both models are tuned on the validation set (months 13–15) using nDCG@10 as the selection metric. Grid search is performed over reasonable hyperparameter ranges (learning rate, regularization strength, model size). The test set is held out and never touched during tuning. Once both models are finalized on validation performance, they are each evaluated exactly once on the test set.

### 7. Evaluation Metrics and Primary/Secondary Measures
**Primary metric:** **Next-day unbiased nDCG@10** on the test set. nDCG is computed from editorial judgments (where available) or propensity-weighted engagement labels (where not), and is reported overall and **separately for cold-start vs. warm items**. Justification: nDCG aligns with business goal (users inspect top-ranked results; higher-ranked relevant items matter more) and is unbiased under position bias correction. Next-day measurement avoids short-term feedback loops.

**Secondary metrics:**
- nDCG@5 (shorter user attention span)
- Recall@10 (useful items in top 10?)
- MRR (mean reciprocal rank of first relevant item)
- **Cold-start item nDCG (stratified):** If transformer excels here and LTR baseline struggles, the hypothesis is supported. If both perform equally on cold-start, the transformer's advantage on warm items may reflect learned popularity.
- **Rare query nDCG:** Performance on queries appearing <5 times in training. This tests the long-tail generalization claim.
- **Kendall's tau correlation against editorial judgments:** On the held-out editorial evaluation set, we measure rank correlation to unbiased ground truth.

### 8. Confound Control and Scope
**Popularity confound:** Cold-start stratification isolates this directly. We report nDCG separately for cold and warm items. A significant gap (transformer strong on cold, weak on warm) would suggest the baseline is learning popularity while the transformer learns semantics.

**Seasonality confound:** Temporal split ensures test data is from a different season than training (months 16–18 vs. 1–12), reducing seasonal overfitting.

**Position bias:** Propensity-weighted loss and metrics (IPS-corrected nDCG) handle this; propensity models are fit on training data only.

**Query frequency:** Rare query performance is explicitly measured and reported.

### 9. What This Experiment Would and Would Not Establish
**Would establish:** Whether the transformer ranker outperforms LTR on next-day nDCG in this marketplace, and whether the advantage holds on cold-start items and rare queries. If so, it supports the hypothesis that semantic encoding helps with long-tail and novel items.

**Would NOT establish:** Causality in a mechanistic sense (e.g., "the transformer learns semantics"). We observe correlation (better ranking on cold-start items) but cannot directly prove the transformer is not learning some other signal (e.g., title length, brand affinity). However, the cold-start and rare-query stratification strongly constrain alternative explanations.

**Scope:** Results apply to this marketplace platform and the 18-month data window. Generalization to other platforms or different query distributions is out of scope.
difficulty_justification: This case is appropriately difficult because both corruptions are subtle but severe, requiring domain insight into both temporal dynamics in e-commerce and the practical meaning of ranking metrics. A surface-level reviewer might miss the temporal leakage due to the presence of cold-start stratification, and accept AUROC as a 'standard' classification metric without considering position sensitivity.

hypothesis: A transformer-based model that jointly encodes the full prior sequence of student quiz attempts and assignment submissions will predict end-of-term course failure more accurately than a logistic regression baseline on engineered aggregate performance features in an online learning platform.
domain: Education analytics for a university LMS with weekly quizzes, assignments, and clickstream activity
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. Logistic Regression for Early Course Failure Detection

### 1. Data and Labeling

This experiment uses historical LMS records spanning 4 academic years (8 semesters) from the university. The dataset includes all timestamped quiz attempts, assignment submissions, grades, and engagement logs for undergraduate courses with enrollment ≥30 students. The outcome is binary: course failure, defined as a final grade of F or official withdrawal. Labels are assigned deterministically at term end.

The dataset will be drawn from production LMS tables with integrity checks to ensure no duplicate records or missing audit trails. Known data quality issues (e.g., late grade uploads, incomplete engagement logs from system outages) are documented and excluded from analysis if they affect >5% of any course's records.

### 2. Split Strategy

Because course failure rates and grading patterns vary materially across semesters, the split will preserve academic-calendar heterogeneity rather than assigning an entire semester to a single partition. The data are divided as follows:
- **Training:** ~60% of student-course pairs
- **Validation:** ~20%
- **Test:** ~20%

Partitioning is performed at the student-course level across all 8 semesters, with stratification by (a) semester index, (b) course subject category (STEM vs. non-STEM), and (c) failure rate quartile. This ensures that train, validation, and test each contain comparable mixtures of term-specific conditions, course difficulty, and class imbalance, reducing the chance that model selection hinges on an anomalous holdout semester.

The model still makes predictions using only information available during the semester (quiz and assignment attempts up to a given week) and is never shown final exam scores or post-term information during training or validation.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Two feature sets are constructed for fair model comparison:

**Baseline Features (Logistic Regression):** Aggregate statistics computed over the full term:
- Quiz: total attempts, mean score, variance in scores, number of near-zero scores
- Assignments: submission rate (completed / assigned), mean score, days between submissions, count of late submissions
- Engagement: total clickstream hours, weeks of active engagement

**Transformer Features (Sequence Model):** Raw event sequences capturing temporal dynamics:
- Per week: event type (quiz/assignment), score, time since last event (in days)
- Sequences are zero-padded to a fixed 15-week length with masking for missing events

Critically, **both feature sets use identical label and split definitions**. No future information (final exam score, end-of-term GPA, or post-course outcomes) is included. The baseline is not handicapped by coarser temporal resolution; both models see the full term's worth of data.

All scaling, encoding, and imputation transformers are fit exclusively on training data and applied without refitting to validation and test data. This prevents data leakage from the validation and test sets into preprocessing parameters.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Proposed Model:** A transformer encoder with 6 layers, 8 attention heads, and model dimension 128. The sequence of (event_type, score, time_delta) tuples is embedded and processed with positional encoding to capture position within the term. The pooled representation is fed to a binary softmax classification head. Training uses focal loss (α=0.25, γ=2.0) to handle class imbalance and focus learning on hard negatives (students who narrowly avoid failure).

**Baseline:** Logistic regression with L2 regularization, trained on the aggregate feature set. The baseline is given equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort: regularization strength *C* is swept over [0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 100] and selected on the validation set. Both models use class weighting inversely proportional to class frequency. This ensures the baseline is not unfairly disadvantaged and represents a sound comparison: the hypothesis is that sequence encoding adds value beyond aggregate statistics, so the correct baseline is aggregate-feature logistic regression, not a stronger sequence model.

### 5. Hyperparameter Selection and Model Validation

All hyperparameter tuning occurs on the validation set using the primary metric (AUROC). Hyperparameters swept include transformer depth, attention heads, learning rate, dropout, focal loss parameters, and baseline logistic regression *C*. The test set is held out completely and evaluated exactly once after model selection is complete.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary Metric:** AUROC, measured on the test set. This provides a stable measure of discrimination across courses whose failure prevalence and intervention capacity can differ substantially by department and semester. Because the study also compares model quality at multiple points during the term, AUROC serves as a consistent basis for model selection before committing to any single operating threshold.

**Secondary Metrics:**
- Recall at 5% FPR: assess how many failing students can be identified under a limited intervention budget
- Precision at 5% FPR: verify resource demand is feasible
- Expected Calibration Error and Brier score: confirm predicted probabilities reflect true failure likelihood (important for high-stakes decisions)
- Stratified recall and FPR by course subject (STEM vs. non-STEM) to verify generalization across course types
- Stratified recall and FPR by course-level failure rate quartile to ensure robust performance across varied course structures
- Recall and FPR computed at multiple points during the term (e.g., end of weeks 4, 8, 12) to measure whether the transformer provides earlier detection

### 7. Confound Controls

Two major confounds are addressed:

**Course-Specific Grading Patterns:** The transformer might trivially outperform the baseline if it learns to predict failure based on course identity or instructor-specific grading rubrics rather than general risk signals. To control this:
- Stratified split ensures all three sets preserve semester mix, course-type distribution, and difficulty structure
- Secondary metrics explicitly report recall/FPR by course subject; if the transformer's advantage disappears on held-out course subjects, this signals overfitting to course-specific patterns
- Post-hoc: fit a logistic regression baseline that includes course ID as an explicit feature; if the transformer's advantage vanishes, this indicates it was learning course patterns, not transferable risk signals
- Attention weight analysis: visualize which quiz and assignment events the transformer prioritizes; confirm focus is on meaningful early-term signals (e.g., quiz failures, engagement dips) rather than course metadata

**Temporal Resolution Parity:** The baseline is not coarsened; both models see the full term's data up to term end. Both use class weighting. This ensures any advantage for the transformer is due to sequence modeling, not privileged information access.

### 8. Reporting

Results are reported with 95% confidence intervals (bootstrapped over 1000 resamples at the student-course level to account for within-course dependence). The test set is never touched during development; all results are reported once after model selection is complete. The design establishes whether the transformer's sequence encoding provides better discrimination and stronger intervention-relevant recall across varied courses and semesters—not whether it learns course-specific shortcuts.

planted_issues.description: The evaluation uses a random stratified split over all semesters instead of a chronological split. This allows models to learn from future semesters during training and validation, violating the assumption that development data must precede test data in a time-based prediction task. As a result, performance estimates are optimistically biased and do not reflect real-world deployment where only past data is available.

The primary evaluation metric is set to AUROC, which measures overall ranking quality across all thresholds, but the operational goal is to identify at-risk students under a constrained intervention budget (5% false-positive rate). AUROC can remain high even if recall is poor in the low-FPR region that matters for real interventions, making it misleading for the stated use case.

must_not_claim.claim: The stratified split across semesters improves generalization by ensuring all sets contain diverse semester types.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: While stratification improves balance, in a temporal prediction task, any mixing of time-ordered data across splits introduces leakage. The correct design must respect time order: training data must strictly precede validation and test data to avoid inflated performance estimates.

must_not_claim.claim: Using AUROC is appropriate because it is a standard, threshold-invariant metric for imbalanced classification.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: While AUROC is common, it is inappropriate here because the stakeholder cares specifically about performance at low false-positive rates. In operational use, only high-precision, high-recall performance at ~5% FPR supports feasible intervention. A model can have strong AUROC but poor recall in the low-FPR regime, making AUROC misaligned with utility.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies temporal leakage due to improper split strategy and argues that performance estimates are inflated because future data influenced model development.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer explains that for early detection in LMS settings, future semesters are not available at training time, so chronological ordering must be preserved.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies the metric mismatch and argues that AUROC does not reflect operational utility because it does not prioritize performance in the low-FPR region required for feasible interventions.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer demonstrates that a model can achieve high AUROC while performing poorly at 5% FPR, citing precision-recall trade-offs and stakeholder resource constraints.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies both flaws and argues that together they undermine confidence in any claimed advantage for the transformer model: performance is inflated by temporal leakage and misjudged by an inappropriate metric.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer shows that both flaws independently bias results and that only fixing both ensures valid conclusions.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. Logistic Regression for Early Course Failure Detection

### 1. Data and Labeling

This experiment uses historical LMS records spanning 4 academic years (8 semesters) from the university. The dataset includes all timestamped quiz attempts, assignment submissions, grades, and engagement logs for undergraduate courses with enrollment ≥30 students. The outcome is binary: course failure, defined as a final grade of F or official withdrawal. Labels are assigned deterministically at term end.

The dataset will be drawn from production LMS tables with integrity checks to ensure no duplicate records or missing audit trails. Known data quality issues (e.g., late grade uploads, incomplete engagement logs from system outages) are documented and excluded from analysis if they affect >5% of any course's records.

### 2. Temporal Split Strategy

Because the hypothesis concerns early detection and timing of interventions, a chronological split is essential to prevent information leakage. The data are divided as follows:
- **Training:** Semesters 1–5 (years 1–2.5), comprising ~60% of student-course pairs
- **Validation:** Semesters 6–7 (year 3), comprising ~20%
- **Test:** Semester 8 (year 4), comprising ~20%

Within each split, records are stratified by (a) course subject category (STEM vs. non-STEM) and (b) failure rate quartile to ensure each split preserves the course-level heterogeneity present in the full dataset. This prevents the model from overfitting to the distribution of easy vs. hard courses in any single year.

No temporal leakage occurs: training data predates validation data, which predates test data. The model makes predictions using only information available during the semester (quiz and assignment attempts up to a given week) and is never shown final exam scores or post-term information during training or validation.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Two feature sets are constructed for fair comparison:

**Baseline Features (Logistic Regression):** Aggregate statistics computed over the full term:
- Quiz: total attempts, mean score, variance in scores, number of near-zero scores
- Assignments: submission rate (completed / assigned), mean score, days between submissions, count of late submissions
- Engagement: total clickstream hours, weeks of active engagement

**Transformer Features (Sequence Model):** Raw event sequences capturing temporal dynamics:
- Per week: event type (quiz/assignment), score, time since last event (in days)
- Sequences are zero-padded to a fixed 15-week length with masking for missing events

Critically, **both feature sets use identical label and temporal split**. No future information (final exam score, end-of-term GPA, or post-course outcomes) is included. The baseline is not handicapped by coarser temporal resolution; both models see the full term's worth of data.

All scaling, encoding, and imputation transformers are fit exclusively on training data (semesters 1–5) and applied without refitting to validation and test data. This prevents data leakage from the validation and test sets into preprocessing parameters.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Proposed Model:** A transformer encoder with 6 layers, 8 attention heads, and model dimension 128. The sequence of (event_type, score, time_delta) tuples is embedded and processed with positional encoding to capture position within the term. The pooled representation is fed to a binary softmax classification head. Training uses focal loss (α=0.25, γ=2.0) to handle class imbalance and focus learning on hard negatives (students who narrowly avoid failure).

**Baseline:** Logistic regression with L2 regularization, trained on the aggregate feature set. The baseline is given equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort: regularization strength *C* is swept over [0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 100] and selected on the validation set. Both models use class weighting inversely proportional to class frequency. This ensures the baseline is not unfairly disadvantaged and represents a sound comparison: the hypothesis is that sequence encoding adds value beyond aggregate statistics, so the correct baseline is aggregate-feature logistic regression, not a stronger sequence model.

### 5. Hyperparameter Selection and Model Validation

All hyperparameter tuning occurs on the validation set (semesters 6–7) using the primary metric (recall at 5% FPR). Hyperparameters swept include transformer depth, attention heads, learning rate, dropout, focal loss parameters, and baseline logistic regression *C*. The test set (semester 8) is held out completely and evaluated exactly once after model selection is complete.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary Metric:** Recall at a fixed false-positive rate of 5%, measured on the test set. This metric operationally aligns with the stakeholder's goal: identify at-risk students early enough for intervention (tutoring, outreach). A 5% FPR corresponds to ~5% of students receiving targeted support—a resource level the university can realistically sustain. Recall at fixed FPR directly measures how many failing students the model catches while maintaining acceptable false-alarm burden.

**Secondary Metrics:**
- AUROC: overall discrimination ability independent of threshold
- Precision at 5% FPR: verify resource demand is feasible
- Expected Calibration Error and Brier score: confirm predicted probabilities reflect true failure likelihood (important for high-stakes decisions)
- Stratified recall and FPR by course subject (STEM vs. non-STEM) to verify generalization across course types
- Stratified recall and FPR by course-level failure rate quartile to ensure robust performance across varied course structures
- Recall and FPR computed at multiple points during the term (e.g., end of weeks 4, 8, 12) to measure whether the transformer provides earlier detection

### 7. Confound Controls

Two major confounds are addressed:

**Course-Specific Grading Patterns:** The transformer might trivially outperform the baseline if it learns to predict failure based on course identity or instructor-specific grading rubrics rather than general risk signals. To control this:
- Stratified split ensures all three sets preserve course-type distribution
- Secondary metrics explicitly report recall/FPR by course subject; if the transformer's advantage disappears on held-out course subjects, this signals overfitting to course-specific patterns
- Post-hoc: fit a logistic regression baseline that includes course ID as an explicit feature; if the transformer's advantage vanishes, this indicates it was learning course patterns, not transferable risk signals
- Attention weight analysis: visualize which quiz and assignment events the transformer prioritizes; confirm focus is on meaningful early-term signals (e.g., quiz failures, engagement dips) rather than course metadata

**Temporal Resolution Parity:** The baseline is not coarsened; both models see the full term's data up to term end. Both use class weighting. This ensures any advantage for the transformer is due to sequence modeling, not privileged information access.

### 8. Reporting

Results are reported with 95% confidence intervals (bootstrapped over 1000 resamples at the student-course level to account for within-course dependence). The test set is never touched during development; all results are reported once after model selection is complete. The design establishes whether the transformer's sequence encoding provides better recall for early intervention across varied courses and semesters—not whether it learns course-specific shortcuts.
difficulty_justification: This case is moderately difficult because both corruptions are methodologically subtle yet high-impact: temporal leakage arises from a split that appears balanced but breaks chronological assumptions, and metric misuse stems from choosing a standard metric (AUROC) that is technically valid but misaligned with operational utility. Correct evaluation requires understanding both temporal integrity and stakeholder decision constraints.

hypothesis: A dual-encoder semantic search model fine-tuned on historical customer-support tickets and product metadata will retrieve the correct help-article more accurately than a BM25 keyword baseline for resolving incoming e-commerce support inquiries.
domain: E-commerce customer support knowledge retrieval for an online retail platform with a searchable help-center and ticketing system
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Semantic Search for E-Commerce Support Ticket Resolution

### 1. Data and Labeling Strategy

We would use 36 months of production support tickets from the online retailer's ticketing system. The dataset comprises customer queries (text, timestamp, product category, issue type), help-center articles (title, body, publication timestamp, category, revision history), and ground-truth resolution labels extracted from agent actions and user interactions.

Ground truth is defined operationally: a (query, article) pair receives a label of 1 if (a) the support agent assigned that article as the resolution, OR (b) the customer clicked the article within 24 hours of ticket creation and did not escalate to a human agent. Pairs where no clear resolution was recorded, or where multiple articles were involved, are excluded. This labeling scheme is realistic and avoids expensive human annotation while remaining faithful to actual business outcomes.

### 2. Temporal Split to Prevent Leakage

Because support content evolves over time (articles are updated, new articles are added, issue patterns change), a standard random split would leak future information into the training window. Instead, we employ a strict temporal split:

- **Training set (T-36 to T-24):** 12 months of tickets, used to fit the dual-encoder model and build the BM25 index.
- **Validation set (T-18 to T-12):** 6 months of tickets, used exclusively for model selection and hyperparameter tuning.
- **Test set (T-6 to T0):** 6 months of tickets (most recent), held as the final evaluation window.

The 6-month gaps between splits (e.g., T-24 to T-18) serve as buffers to ensure articles published in the validation and test windows are not present in the training data. This prevents the model from learning to rank articles that did not exist when queries were issued.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

For the semantic model, features are the raw query and article text. Queries are lowercased, tokenized, and augmented with categorical embeddings for product type and issue category. Articles use their title (weighted 1.5× to emphasize key information) and the first 512 tokens of body text.

Preprocessing (tokenization, vocabulary construction) is established once from the full corpus of available query and article text before model fitting and then applied identically to training, validation, and test sets. This keeps support terminology consistent across time windows, which is important when product names, issue labels, and article revisions drift over the 36-month horizon.

The BM25 baseline uses the same tokenized text with standard BM25 term-weighting parameters (k1=1.5, b=0.75). No hand-crafted similarity features are engineered for either approach, ensuring a fair text-only comparison.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The semantic model is a dual-encoder architecture using two transformer encoders (e.g., DistilBERT) trained jointly via multiple negatives ranking loss on the training set. Fine-tuning proceeds for 3 epochs at learning rate 2e-5 with 500 warmup steps and batch size 64. Checkpoints are saved after each epoch.

The baseline is BM25 (Okapi), the industry standard for keyword-based retrieval. BM25 is indexed over the same training articles and kept at the standard k1=1.5, b=0.75 setting rather than tuned on the validation window, because the comparison is intended to reflect the lightweight lexical retriever used in practice rather than a separately optimized BM25 variant. The hypothesis remains that supervised fine-tuning on historical resolution labels outperforms unsupervised term matching.

### 5. Model Selection and Validation

After each training epoch, both models are evaluated on the validation set using pair-level AUROC over candidate (query, article) pairs. The checkpoint with the highest validation AUROC is retained. This makes model selection less sensitive to variation in candidate-set size across issue types and product categories while still measuring how well the model separates relevant from non-relevant articles.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Pair-level AUROC over candidate (query, article) pairs in the evaluation window. AUROC is computed by scoring the candidate pool for each query and aggregating across queries. This is appropriate here because it measures relevance discrimination independently of any single rank cutoff and remains comparable across support categories where the number of plausible candidate articles differs.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Recall@5: What fraction of queries have the correct article in the top 5?
- Precision@1: What fraction of rank-1 results are correct (top-of-funnel deflection)?
- Mean Rank: Average position of the correct article (interpretability, tail analysis).

### 7. Controlling for Confounds

Three potential confounds are addressed:

1. **Article popularity and recency bias:** The model could improve simply because newer articles (published after the training window) are more popular. We stratify test results by article publication date (trained-era vs. validation-era vs. test-era articles) and report AUROC separately for each stratum. If the semantic model outperforms BM25 consistently across strata, recency is not the driver.

2. **Long-tail query generalization:** Support queries follow a power-law distribution. We stratify test results by query frequency in the training set (head vs. tail queries) to verify the model does not simply memorize frequent patterns.

3. **Category-specific shortcuts:** The model might exploit category information rather than semantic understanding. Results are further stratified by product category to confirm robustness.

### 8. Test Set Evaluation and Reporting

After checkpoint selection on the validation window, we will run a confirmatory evaluation on the test set to ensure the selected epoch remains stable under the most recent query mix before finalizing the training recipe. If the validation-selected checkpoint and a neighboring checkpoint are statistically indistinguishable on validation but differ noticeably on the test window, we will retain the more stable checkpoint for final reporting. Results are then reported as point estimates for AUROC and secondary metrics, with 95% confidence intervals computed via bootstrap resampling (10,000 iterations) of test queries.

### 9. Scope and Limitations

This design establishes that the semantic model outperforms BM25 on this retailer's data during the test period. It does not establish generalization to (1) future time periods beyond T0, (2) other e-commerce domains, or (3) different article update cadences. These would be separate experiments.

planted_issues.description: The preprocessing tokenizer and vocabulary are fit on the full corpus (including validation and test data) before splitting. This leaks future lexical information — such as newly introduced product names and updated support terminology — into the training pipeline, allowing the model to 'see' content that would otherwise be out-of-distribution in a production setting. This undermines temporal integrity and inflates performance estimates.

The primary evaluation metric is set to pair-level AUROC, which measures overall relevance discrimination without regard to rank position. However, the business goal is to surface the correct article in the top 5 results within a search UI. AUROC can improve even if the correct article remains at rank 10 or 50. This misalignment means model improvements may not translate to actual user-facing gains in deflection or resolution speed.

The test set is used to select between validation-equivalent checkpoints based on performance stability, meaning it is no longer an independent evaluation. This introduces test-set feedback into model development, inflating reported performance and breaking the principle of a final, untouched evaluation. The test set is being used for tuning, even if only for final selection.

The BM25 baseline is left untuned and frozen at default parameters, while the semantic model benefits from validation-based model selection. This creates an unfair comparison: gains may reflect superior optimization effort rather than inherent model superiority. To be equitable, both models should either receive equal tuning access or neither should.

must_not_claim.claim: The temporal split introduces large gaps between datasets (6 months), which unnecessarily reduces training data and could harm model performance.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The 6-month buffer gaps are necessary to prevent leakage of articles published in the future into earlier periods. In real-world e-commerce support, new articles and revised content emerge continuously; the buffer ensures the model never sees or ranks articles that weren't yet public when a query occurred. This design improves valid temporal generalization at the cost of data — a justified trade-off.

must_not_claim.claim: Using raw query and article text without hand-crafted features is insufficient for capturing domain-specific signals like product category or issue type.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Categorical embeddings for product type and issue category are already incorporated into the query encoder input. Not engineering additional similarity features preserves a clean comparison between the dual-encoder’s learned representations and BM25’s lexical matching, both operating on the same underlying text — which is appropriate for testing whether fine-tuning improves retrieval over keywords.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic successfully identifies at least the three high-severity issues: preprocessing leakage, metric misalignment with business goals, and test-set contamination. The final verdict concludes that the design as presented cannot reliably evaluate whether the semantic model improves top-5 retrieval accuracy in production.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic explains that the full-corpus preprocessing leaks future vocabulary, that AUROC does not reflect ranking quality at top-k, and that test-set usage in model selection invalidates its role as an independent evaluation.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies the broken baseline as an unfair comparison, noting that model gains might reflect differential tuning effort rather than semantic superiority. A corrected design would either allow BM25 to use the validation set for parameter selection or remove model selection entirely for the semantic model.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic shows that BM25 is frozen while the semantic model benefits from validation-based checkpointing, creating an optimization asymmetry that confounds interpretation of results.

acceptable_resolutions.description: A high-quality critique emphasizes the compounding risk of metric misalignment (AUROC) combined with test-set reuse: selecting checkpoints based on test AUROC may favor models that improve overall relevance scores but not top-5 placement, directly undermining the operational objective.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic articulates that because AUROC ignores rank ordering, a model might improve the metric while degrading MRR@5 or Recall@5; using this flawed metric to guide checkpoint selection on the test set compounds the methodological error.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Semantic Search for E-Commerce Support Ticket Resolution

### 1. Data and Labeling Strategy

We would use 36 months of production support tickets from the online retailer's ticketing system. The dataset comprises customer queries (text, timestamp, product category, issue type), help-center articles (title, body, publication timestamp, category, revision history), and ground-truth resolution labels extracted from agent actions and user interactions.

Ground truth is defined operationally: a (query, article) pair receives a label of 1 if (a) the support agent assigned that article as the resolution, OR (b) the customer clicked the article within 24 hours of ticket creation and did not escalate to a human agent. Pairs where no clear resolution was recorded, or where multiple articles were involved, are excluded. This labeling scheme is realistic and avoids expensive human annotation while remaining faithful to actual business outcomes.

### 2. Temporal Split to Prevent Leakage

Because support content evolves over time (articles are updated, new articles are added, issue patterns change), a standard random split would leak future information into the training window. Instead, we employ a strict temporal split:

- **Training set (T-36 to T-24):** 12 months of tickets, used to fit the dual-encoder model and build the BM25 index.
- **Validation set (T-18 to T-12):** 6 months of tickets, used exclusively for model selection and hyperparameter tuning.
- **Test set (T-6 to T0):** 6 months of tickets (most recent), held untouched until final evaluation.

The 6-month gaps between splits (e.g., T-24 to T-18) serve as buffers to ensure articles published in the validation and test windows are not present in the training data. This prevents the model from learning to rank articles that did not exist when queries were issued.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

For the semantic model, features are the raw query and article text. Queries are lowercased, tokenized, and augmented with categorical embeddings for product type and issue category. Articles use their title (weighted 1.5× to emphasize key information) and the first 512 tokens of body text.

Preprocessing (tokenization, vocabulary construction) is fit on the training set only and then applied identically to validation and test sets. This prevents the model from learning vocabulary from future queries.

The BM25 baseline uses the same tokenized text with standard BM25 term-weighting parameters (k1=1.5, b=0.75). No hand-crafted similarity features are engineered for either approach, ensuring a fair text-only comparison.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The semantic model is a dual-encoder architecture using two transformer encoders (e.g., DistilBERT) trained jointly via multiple negatives ranking loss on the training set. Fine-tuning proceeds for 3 epochs at learning rate 2e-5 with 500 warmup steps and batch size 64. Checkpoints are saved after each epoch.

The baseline is BM25 (Okapi), the industry standard for keyword-based retrieval. BM25 is applied to the same training articles and requires no supervised tuning, making it an honest comparison: the hypothesis is that *supervised fine-tuning* on historical resolution labels outperforms *unsupervised* term matching.

### 5. Model Selection and Validation

After each training epoch, both models are evaluated on the validation set using Mean Reciprocal Rank at k=5 (MRR@5). The checkpoint with the highest validation MRR@5 is retained. This ensures model selection is driven by held-out performance and avoids overfitting to the test set.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Mean Reciprocal Rank at k=5 (MRR@5). MRR aligns directly with the business goal: surface the correct article in the top 5 results. It weights early retrievals more heavily, incentivizing top-1 placement. MRR is robust to the ambiguous, long-tail query distribution inherent in support search.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Recall@5: What fraction of queries have the correct article in the top 5?
- Precision@1: What fraction of rank-1 results are correct (top-of-funnel deflection)?
- Mean Rank: Average position of the correct article (interpretability, tail analysis).

### 7. Controlling for Confounds

Three potential confounds are addressed:

1. **Article popularity and recency bias:** The model could improve simply because newer articles (published after the training window) are more popular. We stratify test results by article publication date (trained-era vs. validation-era vs. test-era articles) and report MRR@5 for each stratum. If the semantic model outperforms BM25 consistently across strata, recency is not the driver.

2. **Long-tail query generalization:** Support queries follow a power-law distribution. We stratify test results by query frequency in the training set (head vs. tail queries) to verify the model does not simply memorize frequent patterns.

3. **Category-specific shortcuts:** The model might exploit category information rather than semantic understanding. Results are further stratified by product category to confirm robustness.

### 8. Test Set Evaluation and Reporting

The test set is evaluated exactly once, after checkpoint selection, using the final model. Results are reported as point estimates for MRR@5 and secondary metrics, with 95% confidence intervals computed via bootstrap resampling (10,000 iterations) of test queries.

### 9. Scope and Limitations

This design establishes that the semantic model outperforms BM25 on this retailer's data during the test period. It does not establish generalization to (1) future time periods beyond T0, (2) other e-commerce domains, or (3) different article update cadences. These would be separate experiments.
difficulty_justification: This case is challenging because three of the four corruptions are subtle or context-dependent, requiring deep understanding of IR evaluation best practices, temporal integrity, and business alignment. The combination of AUROC misuse and test-set reuse is particularly nuanced, as their interaction amplifies the methodological flaw beyond their individual impacts.

hypothesis: For hospital readmission prediction, a sequence model trained on the last 12 months of longitudinal EHR events and medication orders will outperform a tabular model built from aggregated visit-level features in predicting 30-day unplanned readmission, especially for patients with irregular care patterns.
domain: Healthcare / clinical ML — inpatient and outpatient electronic health record data from a large health system
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Sequence vs. Tabular Models for Hospital Readmission Prediction

### 1. Data and Labeling Strategy

The experiment uses 4 years of de-identified EHR data (2020–2023) from a large health system. The index population comprises all inpatient discharges. The outcome is defined as any acute, unplanned readmission (emergency department visit leading to admission or direct inpatient admission) within 30 days of discharge. Planned or scheduled readmissions are excluded by cross-referencing admission type codes with discharge notes. Patients who die or transfer out of the health system's network during the follow-up window are censored and excluded from the analysis, as true readmission status cannot be determined.

### 2. Temporal Split Strategy

A chronological split by discharge date is used to prevent leakage of future clinical information and treatment evolution. The splits are:
- **Training:** Discharges from January 2020 through December 2021 (24 months)
- **Validation:** Discharges from January 2022 through June 2022 (6 months)
- **Test:** Discharges from July 2022 through December 2023 (18 months)

This approach is essential for three reasons. First, clinical practice, medication guidelines, and coding standards evolve over time; training on data from one year and testing on another ensures the model generalizes to changing practice. Second, utilization patterns and acuity trends shift; a sequence model may exploit temporal trends in the training data that do not hold in future periods. Third, a chronological split prevents the model from learning spurious associations between discharge timing and future readmission driven by calendar effects or systematic changes in hospital operations.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

The sequence model operates on event-level raw data from the 12 months prior to discharge: diagnosis codes (ICD-10), procedure codes (CPT), lab results with values and normal-range flags, medication orders (drug, dose, route, frequency), and admission/ED visit timestamps. All events are ordered chronologically to day-level granularity.

The tabular baseline uses aggregated features derived from the same 12-month lookback window: (a) count and temporal recency of diagnoses grouped by Charlson/Elixhauser comorbidity categories; (b) count of distinct medications and medication classes; (c) counts of ED visits, prior admissions, and outpatient encounters; (d) summary statistics (mean, min, max, trend) of key laboratory values (creatinine, hemoglobin, glucose); (e) length of current hospitalization; (f) demographics (age, sex, insurance type). Both models use an identical 12-month lookback period to ensure a fair comparison.

All preprocessing transformers (standardization of lab values, embedding of categorical codes, imputation of missing values) are fit exclusively on the training set and applied identically to validation and test sets. This prevents data leakage from future periods into the training statistics.

### 4. Model Architecture and Baseline

The sequence model is an LSTM with embedding layers for categorical codes (embedding dimension 64), two LSTM layers with 128 hidden units each, dropout of 0.3 to prevent overfitting, and two fully connected layers (64 and 32 units) culminating in a sigmoid output for binary classification. This architecture is chosen to capture long-range temporal dependencies in patient trajectories.

The baseline is logistic regression trained on the aggregated tabular features with L2 regularization. Logistic regression is the appropriate baseline because it is a well-calibrated, interpretable linear model that represents a fair statistical comparison; both the baseline and sequence model use the same input features (aggregated statistics) except for the sequence model's access to raw temporal order. Hyperparameter tuning effort is equivalent: the baseline's regularization strength is tuned via grid search on the validation set.

### 5. Model Selection and Hyperparameter Tuning

Both models undergo 5-fold cross-validation within the training set to select hyperparameters (learning rate, dropout, embedding size for the sequence model; regularization strength for the baseline). Cross-validation is performed on the training set only. The best hyperparameters are then applied to both models, which are evaluated on the validation set. The model achieving the highest AUPRC (defined below) on the validation set is selected as the final model.

### 6. Primary and Secondary Metrics

The primary metric is **Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC).** This choice reflects the stakeholder's operational objective: identifying high-risk patients in the upper tail of the risk distribution to allocate limited care-management resources. Precision-recall is more sensitive to tail performance than AUROC, especially when the baseline readmission rate is low (~15–20%). A clinically meaningful threshold—e.g., flagging the top 20% of patients by predicted risk—should achieve high precision (most flagged patients truly readmit) and reasonable recall (most readmissions are captured).

Secondary metrics include:
- Sensitivity at 80% specificity: the number of readmissions identified when triggering interventions in ~20% of discharges
- Precision at the 20% risk threshold: actionable for resource planning
- AUROC: standard discrimination metric across all thresholds
- Calibration plot (observed vs. predicted readmission rate by risk decile): ensures that predicted probabilities reflect true event rates and can reliably guide threshold-setting

### 7. Test Set Evaluation and Controls for Confounds

The test set is locked during model development and hyperparameter tuning. After final model selection on the validation set, both the sequence model and baseline are re-trained on the combined training + validation data (January 2020–June 2022) to maximize training signal, then evaluated exactly once on the held-out test set. Test results are reported with 95% confidence intervals via stratified bootstrap resampling.

Three key confounds are addressed. First, recency and visit intensity: the tabular baseline includes features for visit frequency and admission count. If the sequence model's gain persists despite the baseline already capturing these features, the improvement is not merely due to better encoding of recent events. Second, comorbidity burden: both models include rich comorbidity features; stratified analyses confirm the sequence model's advantage is not driven by a particular comorbidity stratum. Third, data censoring: subgroup analyses by insurance type and patient retention ensure the result is not an artifact of missing follow-up data in one demographic group.

Final results include subgroup performance (AUPRC stratified by age quartile, comorbidity burden, visit frequency quartiles, and admission reason category) to demonstrate generalization. Any marked performance divergence in specific subgroups is flagged as potentially concerning and investigated further.

must_not_claim.claim: The test set is suspiciously long (18 months) compared to training (24 months) and validation (6 months), suggesting potential data imbalance or inappropriate holdout strategy.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The long test period is intentional and correct: it allows for rigorous evaluation of model generalization to future time periods, which is critical in healthcare where clinical practices evolve. The extended test window captures multiple seasonal trends and ensures sufficient power to detect performance degradation.

must_not_claim.claim: Using AUPRC instead of AUROC is inappropriate because it's less common or harder to interpret.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: AUPRC is the preferred metric in this context because the positive class (readmission) is relatively rare (~15–20%) and the operational goal prioritizes high-precision identification of high-risk patients. AUROC can be overly optimistic in imbalanced settings, while AUPRC better reflects performance in the decision-relevant region of the curve.

must_not_claim.claim: The model selection process uses validation set performance, but then re-trains on training + validation — this introduces contamination because the test set spans a later time period that may reflect different dynamics.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Re-training on training + validation after model selection is a standard and valid practice to maximize data usage, provided the model architecture and hyperparameters were finalized using only the training and validation sets without test set influence. The chronological split ensures temporal integrity, and the test set remains untouched until final evaluation.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The debate concludes that the experimental design is sound and no critical flaws exist. The reviewer raises no valid methodological objections, or any concerns raised are successfully rebutted by the defender with domain-appropriate reasoning.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The defender demonstrates that all design choices align with clinical ML best practices, particularly temporal splitting, leakage prevention, appropriate metric selection, and fair baseline modeling.

acceptable_resolutions.description: A reviewer raises a methodological concern, but the defender provides a principled justification rooted in the problem context (e.g., why AUPRC is better than AUROC, why chronological split is required, why censoring is necessary), and the reviewer accepts the defense as valid.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The concern is based on a general ML heuristic that does not apply in this longitudinal clinical setting, and the defender explains the domain-specific rationale.
