**Temporal drift:** The temporal split exposes both models to the same non-stationary shifts; performance differences reflect modeling choices. We also stratify test performance by early (months 31–33) vs. late (34–36) to confirm neither model systematically favors recent tickets.

**Context as confound:** We run an ablation where a third baseline (TF-IDF on full thread) is trained to isolate whether gains come from context availability or transformer architecture.

**Label imbalance:** Stratified evaluation by category ensures the model does not sacrifice minority classes.

**Repeated users:** We document the proportion of repeat customers in the test set and, if substantial (>20%), report a secondary result for first-ticket-per-customer only to rule out user-memorization effects.

### 8. Scope and Claims
This design tests whether the proposed transformer model, given full thread + history context, ranks categories more accurately than TF-IDF on the current message. It does not claim that transformers are inherently better; it establishes the operational value of thread context and transformer encoding in this specific domain. Results generalize to future tickets in the same marketplace (temporal validity) but may not transfer to different support domains with different taxonomy structures or user behavior.
difficulty_justification: The corruptions are subtle because both the split and metric choices appear defensible at face value—stratification improves coverage, and cost-weighting reflects business impact—but each diverges critically from the operational reality of time-based generalization and top-k ranking, requiring domain-specific reasoning to detect.

hypothesis: A gradient-boosted model trained on sequence-aware claims history and pharmacy refill patterns will predict 90-day medication nonadherence for chronic hypertension patients better than a static demographics-plus-diagnosis baseline, as measured on a future held-out cohort.
domain: Healthcare claims analytics for chronic disease management in a payer or value-based care setting
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Sequence-Aware Gradient Boosting for 90-Day Medication Nonadherence Prediction

### 1. Data Cohort and Label Definition

We would construct a longitudinal cohort from 36 months of health insurer or integrated delivery network claims data (January 2021 through December 2023). The study population includes all patients aged 18 or older with a diagnosis code for hypertension (ICD-10 I10 or equivalent) and at least one filled antihypertensive prescription during the window. For each patient, we define an index date as the first fill of an antihypertensive medication in a 6-month index window (June 2021 through December 2022). This ensures sufficient future time for outcome measurement.

The primary outcome—nonadherence—is operationalized using the standard pharmacy-based measure: proportion of days covered (PDC) < 0.80 during the 90 days following the index date. PDC is calculated from pharmacy claims (fill dates and days-supply). Patients with enrollment gaps ≥30 days during the 90-day follow-up or who switch antihypertensive drug classes are censored and excluded, eliminating confounding from coverage lapses and therapeutic changes.

### 2. Temporal Split Strategy

To avoid leakage of future information into training, we employ a strict chronological split by index date cohort. Patients with index dates between January 2021 and June 2022 (18 months) form the training set (~60% of the labeled cohort). Patients indexed between July 2022 and September 2022 (3 months) form the validation set (~20%). Finally, patients indexed between October 2022 and December 2022 (3 months) form the held-out test set (~20%). This temporal structure ensures that all features and outcomes for a patient are computed using only information available up to and including the index date; no data from the 90-day follow-up window is used during model development.

Within each temporal split, we apply stratified randomization by age decile and hypertension diagnosis recency (diagnosed ≤1 year vs. >1 year before index) to ensure balanced representation of patient risk profiles across train, validation, and test sets.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

All features are designed to capture sequence-aware history and pharmacy patterns, computed using data available before the index date only. The feature set includes: (a) **sequence history**: count and recency of prior antihypertensive fills in the preceding 6 and 12 months; average days-supply per fill; fill regularity (coefficient of variation of inter-fill intervals); count of medication switches in the prior 12 months. (b) **pharmacy patterns**: fill volume at the patient's primary pharmacy; mail-to-retail fill ratio. (c) **claim-derived indicators**: count of hypertension-related outpatient visits in the prior 12 months; count of comorbidity diagnoses (diabetes, hyperlipidemia, chronic kidney disease); count of distinct drug classes filled. (d) **enrollment and demographics**: age at index, sex, months of continuous enrollment prior to index, and indicator for any enrollment gap >14 days in the prior 6 months.

To keep preprocessing parameters stable across adjacent index-date cohorts, imputation values, scaling coefficients, and categorical encoding maps are established once from the full labeled cohort before model fitting and then frozen for all downstream training, validation, and test transformations. This is intended to reduce month-to-month drift in summary statistics such as fill-duration and enrollment patterns, so that performance differences reflect modeling choices rather than small-sample variation in preprocessing estimates.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The proposed model is a gradient-boosted tree ensemble (XGBoost or LightGBM), with hyperparameters tuned on the validation set. Hyperparameter search covers learning rate (0.01–0.1), tree depth (3–8), minimum child weight (5–20), and subsample ratio (0.7–1.0). Early stopping halts training when validation performance plateaus. The final model is retrained on the combined training and validation sets using the selected hyperparameters.

The baseline is logistic regression trained on a deliberately restricted feature set: age, sex, months of continuous enrollment, binary indicator for prior hypertension diagnosis, and count of prior antihypertensive fills in the preceding 6 months. This baseline deliberately omits sequence patterns and pharmacy timing information, isolating the hypothesis that sequence-aware features add predictive value. The baseline receives equivalent tuning effort (regularization strength selected via grid search on the validation set using the primary metric), ensuring a fair comparison.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics and Model Selection

The primary evaluation metric is **area under the precision-recall curve (PR-AUC)**. This metric is chosen because the operational objective—identifying high-risk nonadherent patients for targeted outreach—demands a balance between precision (to avoid overwhelming care management capacity with false positives) and recall (to cover genuinely high-risk patients). PR-AUC is robust to class imbalance, expected in this population (~30–40% nonadherence), and avoids the inflated ROC-AUC scores common in imbalanced settings.

Secondary metrics include sensitivity and specificity at the operating point achieving 80% precision; calibration metrics (Brier score and decile-wise calibration plots) to ensure predicted probabilities reflect true risk; and AUC-ROC for reference.

Model selection is performed on the validation set using PR-AUC. The test set is held out and touched only once at the end to compute final performance metrics with 95% confidence intervals (bootstrap) and statistical significance testing (DeLong test for AUC difference).

### 6. Confound Control and Scope

We address three key confounds. First, longer observation windows could artificially improve predictions; we control by including observation window duration as a feature and stratifying results by enrollment-duration deciles. Second, better handling of missing enrollment data rather than genuine adherence-pattern learning; we control via explicit censoring rules and sensitivity analyses (varying censoring thresholds). Third, sequence features might proxy socioeconomic status rather than capturing adherence mechanics; we control by stratifying performance by proxy SES indicators (mail vs. retail fill ratio) and confirming improvement persists.

Crucially, this experiment demonstrates improved **predictive ranking** but does not establish causality or prove that sequence patterns mechanistically drive nonadherence—only that they improve risk identification relative to static demographics and diagnosis.

planted_issues.description: The experiment estimates preprocessing parameters (such as imputation values and scaling coefficients) using the full labeled cohort, including validation and test data. This introduces information from future patient cohorts into the model training pipeline, violating temporal integrity. In longitudinal healthcare settings, this leaks future distributional trends into model development and inflates performance estimates, making the evaluation unreliable.

must_not_claim.claim: The temporal split uses too little data for validation and test sets (only 3 months each), potentially leading to high variance in evaluation.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Short validation and test periods are appropriate here because the cohort is strictly chronological and designed to simulate real-world deployment over time. The design explicitly uses stratification by age and diagnosis recency to maintain representativeness, and performance is evaluated with confidence intervals to account for uncertainty.

must_not_claim.claim: The baseline model is unfair because it uses fewer features than the proposed model.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The baseline is intentionally restricted to demographic and static clinical features to isolate the incremental value of sequence-aware and pharmacy timing features. It still undergoes equivalent tuning and uses identical data splits and preprocessing, ensuring a fair comparison of feature value, not model capacity.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critique correctly identifies that using full-cohort statistics for preprocessing leaks future information and invalidates the temporal evaluation design. The defense fails to rebut this because no legitimate justification allows future data to inform preprocessing in a chronological split. Final verdict: critique stands.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer explains that estimating imputation or scaling parameters on future-labeled data introduces future distributional information into training, biasing performance estimates upward.

(b) **pharmacy patterns**: fill volume at the patient's primary pharmacy; mail-to-retail fill ratio. (d) **enrollment and demographics**: age at index, sex, months of continuous enrollment prior to index, and indicator for any enrollment gap >14 days in the prior 6 months.

All preprocessing—imputation of missing values (using median fill duration and mode from the training set), scaling, and categorical encoding—is fit on the training set only and then applied identically to validation and test sets. This ensures no information leakage from validation or test distributions.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The proposed model is a gradient-boosted tree ensemble (XGBoost or LightGBM), with hyperparameters tuned on the validation set. The baseline receives equivalent tuning effort (regularization strength selected via grid search on the validation set using the primary metric), ensuring a fair comparison.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics and Model Selection

The primary evaluation metric is **area under the precision-recall curve (PR-AUC)**. This metric is chosen because the operational objective—identifying high-risk nonadherent patients for targeted outreach—demands a balance between precision (to avoid overwhelming care management capacity with false positives) and recall (to cover genuinely high-risk patients). PR-AUC is robust to class imbalance, expected in this population (~30–40% nonadherence), and avoids the inflated ROC-AUC scores common in imbalanced settings.

Secondary metrics include sensitivity and specificity at the operating point achieving 80% precision; calibration metrics (Brier score and decile-wise calibration plots) to ensure predicted probabilities reflect true risk; and AUC-ROC for reference.

### 6. Confound Control and Scope

difficulty_justification: This case is subtly difficult because the corrupted preprocessing strategy appears methodologically sound—it aims to stabilize estimator drift—and is framed with plausible justification. However, in a temporal evaluation, using any future data (even for preprocessing) constitutes leakage, making correct evaluation require domain-aware understanding of chronological integrity over surface-level statistical stability.

hypothesis: Using chronologically ordered page-view and event-sequence embeddings from the first 7 days after signup will predict 30-day paid conversion better than a static engineered-feature baseline for a freemium SaaS product, as measured on future cohorts.
domain: E-commerce / marketplace SaaS subscription conversion analytics
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Sequence Embeddings vs. Engineered Features for 30-Day Conversion Prediction

### 1. Objective
To test whether chronologically ordered sequence embeddings constructed from the first 7 days of post-signup events predict 30-day paid conversion with greater precision-recall performance than a baseline of hand-engineered aggregate features, when evaluated on future user cohorts.

### 2. Data and Cohort Definition
The experiment uses 24 months of production user event logs from the freemium SaaS platform. Users are organized into weekly signup cohorts (7-day windows). Each user record includes: signup timestamp and source channel, chronologically ordered events (page views, feature clicks) with millisecond timestamps from the first 30 days post-signup, account attributes (company size, plan tier, geography), and a binary conversion label (paid subscription within 30 days, pulled from billing records).

### 3. Temporal Split Strategy
A cohort-based temporal split is used to prevent leakage and respect the time-series structure of the data:

- **Training cohorts:** Weeks 1–52 (months 1–12). These cohorts are used to fit all models.
- **Validation cohorts:** Weeks 53–65 (months 13–15). Used exclusively for hyperparameter tuning and model selection; no supervised model training occurs here.
- **Test cohorts:** Weeks 66–78 (months 16–18). Held out and evaluated once at the end.

Within each split, users are stratified by signup channel (organic, paid search, partner referral, etc.) to ensure marketing mix does not shift across splits and does not confound the comparison.

This temporal design prevents the model from learning seasonal patterns, marketing dynamics, or product changes that occur between months 1 and 18. The model is trained on the past, validated on the intermediate future, and tested on the distant future—reflecting realistic deployment.

### 4. Feature Engineering
Two parallel feature sets are constructed from identical raw data (first 7 days post-signup only):

**Treatment: Sequence Embeddings**
- Extract chronologically ordered events (event type, timestamp, contextual attributes) from days 1–7.
- Encode this sequence using a learned sequence encoder (e.g., Transformer or LSTM), trained to model event sequences.
- Aggregate the learned embeddings: concatenate [first-event embedding, mean embedding, max embedding, last-event embedding, sequence length].
- Combine with static features (signup source, company size, plan tier, geography).
- Total dimensionality: ~128–256, tuned on validation data.

**Baseline: Engineered Features**
- Compute hand-crafted statistics from the same 7-day window: event count, unique page categories, feature-click frequency, session count, time-to-first-interaction, max inter-event gap, signup-to-first-event latency, event-type entropy.
- Combine with identical static features.
- Total dimensionality: ~20–30.

To keep the representation space consistent across cohort blocks, preprocessing artifacts are established once and then frozen for all experiments. The sequence encoder vocabulary, embedding token statistics, and continuous-feature scaling parameters are derived from the complete study window so that rare event codes and scaling constants do not shift between training, validation, and test cohorts. The resulting embedders and scalers are then applied identically to each split, ensuring that the comparison between embedding-based and engineered features reflects the modeling choice rather than split-specific preprocessing variation.

### 5. Model and Baseline
Both feature sets are trained using logistic regression with L2 regularization. Hyperparameters (regularization strength C, class weights) are selected via grid search on validation cohorts, using the primary metric (Precision@Recall=0.60). The same tuning effort is applied to both models to ensure a fair comparison. The engineered-feature model serves as the baseline; the treatment is the embedding-based model.

A secondary sanity check: train gradient boosted trees (XGBoost) on both feature sets to confirm that the embedding advantage, if present, is not an artifact of the logistic regression model class.

### 6. Primary Metric: Precision at Fixed Recall
The primary metric is **Precision@Recall=0.60**, computed on test cohorts.

**Justification:** The stakeholder's goal is to target high-probability converters within a fixed budget. If the outreach budget allows reaching 60% of all true converters (recall=0.60), precision at that point tells us: of every 100 targeted users, how many will convert? This directly translates to cost per acquisition and campaign efficiency. AUC or accuracy does not capture this operational constraint. Precision@Recall=0.60 isolates the high-confidence region of the model and reflects real-world usage.

### 7. Secondary Metrics
- **Precision@Recall={0.40, 0.80}:** Robustness across different operating points.
- **AUPRC (Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve):** Integrates performance across all thresholds.
- **Calibration (Expected Calibration Error):** Ensures predicted probabilities are reliable for downstream decision-making.
- **Lift vs. random:** (Model precision at recall R) / (baseline conversion rate), showing multiplier gain over untargeted outreach.

### 8. Model Selection and Validation Procedure
1. Train candidate models (embedding + engineered baseline) on training cohorts.
2. Evaluate on validation cohorts using Precision@Recall=0.60.
3. Select the hyperparameter set with highest validation metric.
4. Retrain final models on combined training + validation cohorts.
5. Evaluate on held-out test cohorts once; report all metrics.

### 9. Confound Control
- **Temporal confounding:** Temporal split ensures evaluation on truly future cohorts, ruling out seasonal memorization.
- **Channel mix:** Stratified split controls for marketing channel shifts.
- **Leakage from future events:** Only days 1–7 events used; audit pipeline to confirm no days 8–30 data enters features.
- **Class imbalance:** Class-weighted logistic regression; primary metric (Precision@Recall) automatically handles imbalance.
- **Feature space stability:** Freezing preprocessing artifacts across all cohort blocks keeps tokenization and scaling aligned, reducing the chance that observed differences are driven by representation drift rather than by the feature design itself.
- **Feature leakage isolation:** If embeddings outperform baselines, compare SHAP importances and train variants with event-count features equalized to confirm embeddings capture sequence structure, not just richer signal volume.

### 10. Scope of Claim
This experiment establishes whether learned embeddings outperform engineered features on future cohorts, under equivalent tuning and using identical temporal windows. It does NOT establish causality, global optimality of the embedding architecture, or robustness to post-signup interventions (e.g., email campaigns triggered by day-7 behavior). Results are specific to this SaaS product and may not generalize to different products or data regimes.

planted_issues.description: The preprocessing artifacts (sequence encoder vocabulary, token statistics, scaling parameters) are derived from the full set of cohorts, including validation and test data, which violates the temporal integrity of the evaluation. In a time-based cohort experiment, any preprocessing step that uses future cohorts leaks information about future data distributions, which can subtly bias the embedding space and scaling, resulting in over-optimistic performance estimates on the test set.

must_not_claim.claim: The temporal split is invalid because the validation and test cohorts are too close in time and may share seasonal patterns.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The temporal split is appropriate and realistic: training on early cohorts, validation on intermediate, and test on later cohorts reflects how models are deployed in production. A clean break in time is not required—what matters is that no future data informs training, which would be violated by the preprocessing leakage, not by calendar proximity.

must_not_claim.claim: Freezing preprocessing across splits is always invalid.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Freezing preprocessing is often good practice when no temporal dependence exists. In non-temporal settings, fitting on full data before splitting is acceptable. The flaw here is not the freezing *itself*, but that the data used to fit includes future cohorts in a time-ordered evaluation setup.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer correctly identifies that using the full cohort window (including future data) to fit preprocessing components introduces temporal leakage. The defender cannot validly argue that this stabilizes features, because in a temporally valid setup, future distributional shifts should not inform training. The correct resolution is to recompute preprocessing only on training cohorts and apply it to later splits.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic shows that preprocessing informed by future cohorts can lead to optimistic bias, especially in evolving user behavior or rare event representation, and that the sound design requires fitting only on training data.

hypothesis: For a bank’s card-fraud detection system, a sequence model trained on raw transaction event histories and merchant embeddings will detect fraud more accurately than a gradient-boosted model trained on hand-engineered aggregate features, as measured by out-of-time precision-recall performance on live transactions.

task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Sequence vs. Gradient-Boosted Models for Card Fraud Detection

### 1. Data and Labeling

We would use 24 months of production transaction records from the card issuer's core systems. The dataset includes millions of transactions with associated metadata: amount, merchant category code (MCC), device fingerprint, IP geolocation, timestamp, and chargeback outcomes filed within 60 days of transaction. The fraud label is binary: a transaction is marked fraudulent if a chargeback was filed and adjudicated as fraud by the dispute team; otherwise it is legitimate. Transactions occurring in the final 60 days of the dataset are excluded to ensure label completeness. We expect class imbalance of 0.1–0.5% fraud rate, typical for card fraud portfolios.

### 2. Data Partitioning and Leakage Prevention

The labeled transaction pool is divided into three partitions using stratification by fraud label and transaction month:
- **Training set (70%):** Used to fit models and learn sequence representations.
- **Validation set (15%):** Used for hyperparameter tuning and model selection via Bayesian optimization.
- **Test set (15%):** Held-out for final evaluation.

This partitioning keeps each split comparable in both fraud prevalence and seasonal transaction mix, which is important because PR-style discrimination estimates can vary materially when a holdout happens to overrepresent a small number of issuer campaigns, merchant spikes, or holiday periods. By ensuring that each partition contains the full range of month-level behavior, we can compare the sequence model and baseline under matched class balance and market composition rather than allowing a single contiguous window to dominate the conclusion.

### 3. Feature Construction

**For the sequence model:** We construct a sequence of up to 50 prior transactions per card member from a 90-day lookback window. Each transaction in the sequence is represented by (a) transaction amount, (b) time delta in minutes to the current transaction, (c) merchant category code (embedded as a learned 32-dimensional vector), and (d) transaction channel (one-hot encoded). These historical transactions are padded or truncated to length 50. The current transaction is appended with the same feature representation.

**For the baseline (XGBoost) model:** We construct hand-engineered aggregate features over the same 90-day window: transaction count, sum/mean/std of amounts, unique merchant count, unique geographies, time-to-last-transaction, day-of-week, hour-of-day, and device reuse frequency. The current transaction amount and MCC are also included. Merchant category codes are embedded using the same learned embedding as the sequence model (to ensure the baseline has access to equivalent categorical information).

To keep partition-specific frequency shifts from introducing unnecessary instability into the feature pipeline, scaling statistics and categorical vocabularies are fixed once from the complete labeled transaction pool and then applied unchanged in training, validation, and test transformation. Merchant embeddings for the sequence model are still learned during model training, and no explicitly post-transaction features are included in either model input.

### 4. Model Training and Baseline Comparison

**Sequence model:** An LSTM or Transformer encoder (2–3 layers, 64–128 hidden units) is trained end-to-end on the training set with learned merchant embeddings. A classification head (dense layer + sigmoid activation) produces fraud probability. Because architecture depth, hidden width, and optimization settings can materially affect sequence performance, this model receives Bayesian hyperparameter optimization on the validation partition.

**Baseline model:** XGBoost classifier trained on the hand-engineered features, using a fixed conservative configuration rather than an extensive hyperparameter search. This keeps the non-sequential reference model in a stable regime and avoids tailoring the baseline too closely to a particular validation partition.

Both models use the same class-weight balancing to handle imbalance. The comparison is intended to remain structurally fair because the baseline has access to full transaction history, merchant embeddings, and temporal metadata—the main difference is how history is aggregated. This keeps the focus on whether sequential modeling captures fraud patterns better than engineered aggregates.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Expected Calibration Error (ECE) on the test set. This is appropriate because the output score will ultimately feed downstream risk policies and analyst review queues, where the usefulness of the model depends on whether a predicted fraud probability corresponds to a reliable observed risk level. A model with better probability alignment is easier to operationalize across changing manual-review capacity and rule overlays than one that only improves ranking at a narrow operating point.

**Secondary metrics:** Precision-Recall AUC; recall at 95% precision and precision at 90% recall (operational points); ROC-AUC (sanity check); and stratified recall by merchant category and geography (to detect if gains are concentrated in specific transaction segments).

### 6. Model Selection and Test Hygiene

Hyperparameter tuning and model selection occur only on the validation partition. The test set remains held-out until final evaluation and is used exactly once, with no further tuning or threshold adjustment. This preserves a clean final estimate of how the selected model behaves on unseen transactions.

### 7. Confound Controls

We address several potential confounds:
- **Seasonal composition differences:** Stratified partitioning ensures that both models are evaluated on comparable fraud prevalence and month-level transaction mix rather than on markedly different seasonal slices.
- **Feature richness bias:** The baseline receives access to the full transaction history and embeddings; the only difference is aggregation strategy.
- **Segment-specific gains:** Stratified metrics check whether improvements generalize across merchant categories and geographies or are driven by narrow subsets.
- **Label noise:** Transactions within the final 60 days are excluded; residual label noise affects both models equally.
- **Probability usability:** Calibration is treated as the primary comparison axis so that any observed gain reflects a score that can be used consistently in downstream policy.

This design establishes whether the sequence model yields more decision-ready fraud risk estimates than engineered aggregates, while also showing whether any gains in discrimination are broad-based or confined to particular transaction segments.

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses a stratified but random transaction-level split across time rather than a chronological temporal split. This allows future data to influence training, introducing temporal leakage. In fraud detection, where fraud patterns evolve adversarially and labels mature over time, evaluating on randomly shuffled data does not reflect real-world deployment and inflates performance estimates.

Scaling statistics and categorical vocabularies are computed on the full dataset before partitioning, including data from the future. This leaks distributional information from validation and test periods into the training pipeline, which violates temporal integrity. In a time-sensitive domain like fraud, this can bias model evaluation by allowing the training process to implicitly adapt to future patterns.

The primary evaluation metric is Expected Calibration Error (ECE), which measures probability reliability, but the hypothesis is about fraud detection accuracy under imbalance, best measured by PR-AUC. A model can be well-calibrated but still fail to rank frauds effectively. Optimizing for calibration instead of discrimination misaligns evaluation with the operational goal of catching more fraud at low false-positive rates.

The baseline XGBoost model uses a fixed conservative configuration while the sequence model receives full Bayesian hyperparameter optimization. This asymmetry in tuning effort introduces bias: observed gains may reflect better optimization rather than superior modeling capability, undermining the fairness of the comparison.

must_not_claim.claim: The exclusion of the final 60 days of transactions introduces unnecessary data loss and reduces statistical power.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Excluding the final 60 days is necessary to ensure chargeback labels are mature and complete. Including them would introduce label noise, as some fraudulent transactions may not yet have resulted in chargebacks, and this affects both models equally.

must_not_claim.claim: Using stratified sampling by month and fraud label is appropriate and improves representativeness.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: While stratification controls for seasonal and class balance effects, it does not excuse breaking temporal order. In fraud detection, chronological separation is essential to simulate deployment on future data, and random mixing of time points invalidates out-of-time generalization claims.

must_not_claim.claim: Fixing preprocessing parameters globally improves pipeline stability and reduces variance.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: While stable preprocessing is valuable, it must be fit only on training data. Using global statistics leaks information from future periods and violates the temporal boundary, which is critical in this domain.

must_not_claim.claim: Prioritizing calibration over discrimination makes the model more usable in downstream risk systems.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: While calibration matters, the hypothesis explicitly evaluates detection accuracy under imbalance. A model can be well-calibrated but still fail to prioritize frauds. The primary metric must reflect the hypothesis: improved discrimination via PR-AUC.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic correctly identifies that the random temporal split enables leakage of future patterns into training, invalidating the out-of-time evaluation premise. The defender cannot rebut this — chronological separation is non-negotiable in this domain.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic cites the violation of temporal dependency and explains that fraud patterns evolve over time.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic exposes that preprocessing uses global statistics from the full dataset, leaking validation and test information into training. This undermines the integrity of feature scaling and vocabulary construction in a time-sensitive setting.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic points out that fitting preprocessing on future data violates the no-leakage principle and allows the model to implicitly adapt to unseen patterns.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic argues that ECE is inappropriate as the primary metric because the hypothesis concerns fraud detection performance under imbalance, which is best evaluated with PR-AUC. Relying on calibration risks rewarding well-behaved but non-discriminative models.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic explains that the business goal is to catch more frauds at low false-positive rates, which is directly captured by PR-AUC, not ECE.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic demonstrates that the baseline model receives less tuning effort than the sequence model, introducing confounding. A fair comparison requires equal optimization budgets to isolate the effect of modeling approach.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic notes the asymmetry in hyperparameter search and argues that gains could stem from optimization rather than sequential structure.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Sequence vs. Gradient-Boosted Models for Card Fraud Detection

### 1. Data and Labeling

We would use 24 months of production transaction records from the card issuer's core systems. The dataset includes millions of transactions with associated metadata: amount, merchant category code (MCC), device fingerprint, IP geolocation, timestamp, and chargeback outcomes filed within 60 days of transaction. The fraud label is binary: a transaction is marked fraudulent if a chargeback was filed and adjudicated as fraud by the dispute team; otherwise it is legitimate. Transactions occurring in the final 60 days of the dataset are excluded to ensure label completeness. We expect class imbalance of 0.1–0.5% fraud rate, typical for card fraud portfolios.

### 2. Temporal Split and Leakage Prevention

The data is divided into three chronologically consecutive periods:
- **Training set (months 1–12):** Used to fit all preprocessing transformers, train models, and learn embeddings.
- **Validation set (months 13–18):** Used for hyperparameter tuning and model selection via Bayesian optimization.
- **Test set (months 19–24):** Held-out, touched only once at the end for final evaluation.

This temporal split is critical because it prevents leakage of future chargeback outcomes and allows us to evaluate both models on truly out-of-time data where fraud patterns may have shifted. Random shuffling would break the temporal dependence structure and would not reflect how the model performs in deployment.

### 3. Feature Construction

**For the sequence model:** We construct a sequence of up to 50 prior transactions per card member from a 90-day lookback window. Each transaction in the sequence is represented by (a) transaction amount (scaled using training-set statistics), (b) time delta in minutes to the current transaction, (c) merchant category code (embedded as a learned 32-dimensional vector), and (d) transaction channel (one-hot encoded). These historical transactions are padded or truncated to length 50. The current transaction is appended with the same feature representation.

**For the baseline (XGBoost) model:** We construct hand-engineered aggregate features over the same 90-day window: transaction count, sum/mean/std of amounts, unique merchant count, unique geographies, time-to-last-transaction, day-of-week, hour-of-day, and device reuse frequency. The current transaction amount and MCC are also included. Merchant category codes are embedded using the same learned embedding as the sequence model (to ensure the baseline has access to equivalent categorical information). All numeric features are scaled using training-set statistics.

Critically, all preprocessing (scaling, embedding, encoding) is fit on the training set only and applied identically to validation and test sets. No future or post-transaction information is used.

### 4. Model Training and Baseline Comparison

**Sequence model:** An LSTM or Transformer encoder (2–3 layers, 64–128 hidden units) is trained end-to-end on the training set with learned merchant embeddings. A classification head (dense layer + sigmoid activation) produces fraud probability.

**Baseline model:** XGBoost classifier trained on the hand-engineered features, with identical hyperparameter search budget: 100 Bayesian optimization trials on validation PR-AUC, with early stopping.

Both models use the same class-weight balancing to handle imbalance. The comparison is fair because the baseline has access to full transaction history, merchant embeddings, and temporal metadata—the only structural difference is how history is aggregated. This isolates the hypothesis: does sequential modeling capture fraud patterns better than engineered aggregates?

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Precision-Recall AUC (PR-AUC) on the test set. This is appropriate because (1) fraud is imbalanced, making PR-AUC more informative than ROC-AUC; (2) the business goal is to maximize recall at low false-positive rates, a trade-off PR-AUC directly captures; (3) evaluating on temporally held-out data (out-of-time) reflects deployment conditions.

**Secondary metrics:** Recall at 95% precision and recall at 90% precision (operational points); ROC-AUC (sanity check); Expected Calibration Error (both models must be well-calibrated for operational use); stratified recall by merchant category and geography (to detect if gains are confounded by specific transaction segments).

### 6. Model Selection and Test Hygiene

Hyperparameter tuning and model selection occur only on the validation set (months 13–18). The test set is held-out until final evaluation and is used exactly once, with no further tuning or threshold adjustment. This prevents overfitting and ensures honest out-of-time performance estimates.

### 7. Confound Controls

We address several potential confounds:
- **Temporal drift:** Both models are evaluated on the same future period, so recency effects are visible equally.
- **Feature richness bias:** The baseline receives access to the full transaction history and embeddings; the only difference is aggregation strategy.
- **Segment-specific gains:** Stratified metrics check whether improvements generalize across merchant categories and geographies or are driven by narrow subsets.
- **Label noise:** Transactions within the final 60 days are excluded; residual label noise affects both models equally.
- **Tuning bias:** Both models receive identical hyperparameter search budgets.

This design establishes whether the sequence model's superior PR-AUC is due to capturing sequential fraud patterns or due to confounds (recency, richer feature access, segment-specific behavior, or tuning asymmetry).
difficulty_justification: This case is challenging because multiple subtle corruptions — particularly temporal and preprocessing leakage — can appear as reasonable engineering trade-offs. The metric mismatch and broken baseline further compound the difficulty, requiring a critic to align methodology with the operational hypothesis rather than surface-level plausibility.

hypothesis: For hospital sepsis prediction, a transformer model trained on irregularly sampled longitudinal EHR sequences of vitals, labs, medications, and nursing events will predict 6-hour onset of sepsis more accurately than a gradient-boosted model trained on hand-engineered summary features, when evaluated on future admissions from the same health system.
domain: Acute care hospital electronic health records for sepsis surveillance and early warning
task_prompt:
# Experiment Design: Transformer vs. Gradient-Boosted Models for Sepsis Prediction

## 1. Data and Labeling Strategy

We would use 4 years of EHR data from a single health system, comprising all ICU and general-ward admissions with complete vital signs, laboratory results, medication records, and nursing event documentation. Admissions are included only if the patient has at least 24 hours of prior observations, ensuring adequate historical context.

Sepsis onset is labeled using a clinical definition that requires three elements to co-occur within a 6-hour window: (1) blood culture ordering or collection, (2) broad-spectrum antibiotic initiation, and (3) at least one sign of acute physiologic derangement (systolic BP ≤90 mmHg, lactate ≥4 mmol/L, or acute organ dysfunction per SOFA). This definition is retrospectively applied using charted data only and is frozen before model training. To mitigate label ambiguity, any disagreement between two clinical reviewers is resolved by exclusion; the remaining dataset reflects consensus labels. Admissions with sepsis diagnosed in the first 24 hours are excluded to avoid pre-existing disease.

## 2. Temporal Split and Leakage Prevention

The dataset is divided chronologically without overlap: months 1–24 for training, months 25–30 for validation, and months 31–36 for held-out test evaluation. This temporal ordering reflects the deployment scenario and prevents leakage from treatment escalation patterns that accumulate over a patient's ICU stay.

Within each split, admissions are ordered by timestamp without random shuffling. Stratification by admission type (ICU vs. general ward) ensures consistent case mix across splits and allows detection of unit-specific documentation biases.

## 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

For the transformer model, we preserve raw, timestamped sequences of vitals (HR, BP, RR, SpO2, temperature), labs (lactate, creatinine, WBC, platelet, hemoglobin, bilirubin, glucose), medication administrations (name, dose, route, timestamp), and nursing events (assessments and interventions). Sequences are truncated to the 72 hours preceding the sepsis determination period and extended through the 6-hour prediction interval for measurements that are already documented in routine care, so the model can use the full evolving physiologic trajectory available at the bedside. No hand-engineered summary features are constructed for the transformer.

For the baseline gradient-boosted model, we engineer summary statistics over the same observation horizon: minimum, maximum, mean, and standard deviation of vitals and labs; medication order counts by category; nursing event recency and frequency. Static features (age, sex, admission diagnosis, Charlson index) are included in both models.

All preprocessing transformers (imputation strategies, scaling parameters, categorical encodings) are fit exclusively on the training set and applied identically to validation and test sets without refitting. This prevents information leakage from validation and test distributions.

## 4. Model Architectures and Baseline

The transformer model consists of 4 attention heads, 2 transformer blocks, 256 hidden dimensions, layer normalization, and 0.2 dropout. Positional encoding is learned from timestamps. The model outputs a single binary logit for sepsis onset within 6 hours.

The baseline is a gradient-boosted tree model (XGBoost or LightGBM) trained on hand-engineered summary features. Both models undergo equivalent hyperparameter tuning via grid search on the validation set. The baseline receives the same class balancing treatment and threshold adjustment strategy as the transformer. Both are evaluated on the identical test set using the same preprocessing. This ensures that any performance difference reflects the transformer's capacity to exploit irregular sequences, not differential tuning or feature access.

## 5. Evaluation Metrics and Operating Point

The primary metric is precision at a fixed recall threshold of ≥80%. This reflects operational reality: clinicians must catch most sepsis cases to intervene early. The question is whether the transformer achieves higher precision (fewer false alarms) than the baseline at this mandatory recall floor, reducing alert fatigue. We also report the area under the precision-recall curve (AUPRC) across all operating points, which is less sensitive to class imbalance than AUROC.

Secondary metrics include AUROC, F1 score, sensitivity, specificity, alert lead time (median time from first alert to sepsis onset for true positives), and calibration measures (expected calibration error, Brier score). Lead time is clinically important; it quantifies the actionable advance notice the model provides.

## 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameters are selected using the validation set (months 25–30) only, via a nested split: 80% for tuning, 20% for selection. The final model configuration is chosen based on AUPRC on the selection subset. The test set (months 31–36) is never examined during tuning and is used only once, at the end, to report final metrics.

## 7. Confound Controls and Scope

To address the critique that performance gains come from exploit of timestamp resolution or missingness patterns rather than better clinical signal, we employ several controls: (1) both models operate on the same 72-hour lookback and same logical information set; (2) performance is reported separately for ICU and general-ward admissions to detect documentation biases; and (3) manual review of discordant predictions on a held-out sample of test admissions assesses label quality.

This design establishes whether the transformer outperforms the baseline in real operational conditions (future admissions, temporal ordering preserved). It does not establish superiority on other health systems, different sepsis definitions, or different clinical populations without additional validation studies.

planted_issues.description: The model inputs include medication administrations and other clinical events that extend into the 6-hour prediction window, during which the sepsis label is determined. Since the label requires broad-spectrum antibiotic initiation within that window, allowing antibiotic administration as a feature means the model can directly observe a component of its own target, resulting in target leakage. This inflates performance artificially and invalidates the claim of early prediction.

must_not_claim.claim: The temporal split is invalid because it does not use random sampling or cross-validation.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Temporal splits are appropriate and necessary in clinical forecasting to reflect real-world deployment where future admissions are predicted after model training. Random shuffling would induce time-based leakage and invalidate generalization claims to future patients.

must_not_claim.claim: Excluding admissions with label disagreement reduces statistical power and biases the dataset.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Label ambiguity is a major concern in clinical outcome definitions. Requiring consensus ensures label reliability for a high-stakes prediction task. The trade-off between sample size and label quality is appropriate here, especially when combined with a manual review of discordant predictions.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic correctly identifies that including medication administrations extending through the prediction window introduces target leakage, as antibiotic initiation is both a feature and part of the label definition. The final verdict must conclude that the experiment overestimates model performance and requires re-design with strict input masking before the prediction horizon.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic demonstrates that a core component of the label (antibiotic initiation) is allowed as input to the model, violating the principle that features must be fully available prior to the outcome window.

## 1. Data and Labeling Strategy

Admissions are included only if the patient has at least 24 hours of prior observations, ensuring adequate historical context.

Sepsis onset is labeled using a clinical definition that requires three elements to co-occur within a 6-hour window: (1) blood culture ordering or collection, (2) broad-spectrum antibiotic initiation, and (3) at least one sign of acute physiologic derangement (systolic BP ≤90 mmHg, lactate ≥4 mmol/L, or acute organ dysfunction per SOFA). This definition is retrospectively applied using charted data only and is frozen before model training. To mitigate label ambiguity, any disagreement between two clinical reviewers is resolved by exclusion; the remaining dataset reflects consensus labels. Admissions with sepsis diagnosed in the first 24 hours are excluded to avoid pre-existing disease.

## 2. Temporal Split and Leakage Prevention

Critically, antibiotic administration is not used as a feature input to either model, preventing the models from exploiting treatment decisions rather than detecting physiologic deterioration.

Within each split, admissions are ordered by timestamp without random shuffling. Stratification by admission type (ICU vs. general ward) ensures consistent case mix across splits and allows detection of unit-specific documentation biases.

## 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

For the transformer model, we preserve raw, timestamped sequences of vitals (HR, BP, RR, SpO2, temperature), labs (lactate, creatinine, WBC, platelet, hemoglobin, bilirubin, glucose), medication administrations (name, dose, route, timestamp), and nursing events (assessments and interventions). Sequences are truncated to 72 hours prior to the prediction window and padded with a special token where observations are missing. No hand-engineered summary features are constructed for the transformer.

For the baseline gradient-boosted model, we engineer summary statistics over the same 72-hour lookback: minimum, maximum, mean, and standard deviation of vitals and labs; medication order counts by category; nursing event recency and frequency. Static features (age, sex, admission diagnosis, Charlson index) are included in both models.

All preprocessing transformers (imputation strategies, scaling parameters, categorical encodings) are fit exclusively on the training set and applied identically to validation and test sets without refitting. This prevents information leakage from validation and test distributions.

## 4. Model Architectures and Baseline

The transformer model consists of 4 attention heads, 2 transformer blocks, 256 hidden dimensions, layer normalization, and 0.2 dropout. Positional encoding is learned from timestamps. The model outputs a single binary logit for sepsis onset within 6 hours.

The baseline is a gradient-boosted tree model (XGBoost or LightGBM) trained on hand-engineered summary features. Both models undergo equivalent hyperparameter tuning via grid search on the validation set. The baseline receives the same class balancing treatment and threshold adjustment strategy as the transformer. Both are evaluated on the identical test set using the same preprocessing. This ensures that any performance difference reflects the transformer's capacity to exploit irregular sequences, not differential tuning or feature access.

## 5. Evaluation Metrics and Operating Point

The primary metric is precision at a fixed recall threshold of ≥80%. This reflects operational reality: clinicians must catch most sepsis cases to intervene early. The question is whether the transformer achieves higher precision (fewer false alarms) than the baseline at this mandatory recall floor, reducing alert fatigue. We also report the area under the precision-recall curve (AUPRC) across all operating points, which is less sensitive to class imbalance than AUROC.

Secondary metrics include AUROC, F1 score, sensitivity, specificity, alert lead time (median time from first alert to sepsis onset for true positives), and calibration measures (expected calibration error, Brier score). Lead time is clinically important; it quantifies the actionable advance notice the model provides.

## 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameters are selected using the validation set (months 25–30) only, via a nested split: 80% for tuning, 20% for selection. The final model configuration is chosen based on AUPRC on the selection subset. The test set (months 31–36) is never examined during tuning and is used only once, at the end, to report final metrics.

## 7. Confound Controls and Scope

To address the critique that performance gains come from exploit of timestamp resolution or missingness patterns rather than better clinical signal, we employ several controls: (1) both models operate on the same 72-hour lookback and same logical information set; (2) antibiotic administration is explicitly excluded as a feature to prevent learning of treatment escalation rather than physiologic change; (3) performance is reported separately for ICU and general-ward admissions to detect documentation biases; and (4) manual review of discordant predictions on a held-out sample of test admissions assesses label quality.

This design establishes whether the transformer outperforms the baseline in real operational conditions (future admissions, temporal ordering preserved). It does not establish superiority on other health systems, different sepsis definitions, or different clinical populations without additional validation studies.
difficulty_justification: The flaw is subtle because including real-time clinical events like medication administration seems intuitively useful and clinically justified, but the critical insight is recognizing that one of those events (antibiotic initiation) is also part of the label definition, creating target leakage. This requires precise alignment between label construction and feature eligibility.

hypothesis: A sequence model trained on raw clickstream and search-session events will predict next-session purchase conversion better than a gradient-boosted model using aggregated user-level engagement features for a large e-commerce marketplace, when both are evaluated on future weeks of traffic.
domain: E-commerce marketplace customer behavior modeling
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Sequence Models vs. Gradient-Boosted Models for E-Commerce Conversion Prediction

### 1. Objective and Hypothesis

This experiment tests whether a sequence model trained on raw clickstream and search-session events predicts next-session purchase conversion better than a gradient-boosted model using aggregated user-level engagement features. Both models are evaluated on future traffic to ensure results reflect genuine predictive performance, not training-set artifacts.

### 2. Data Strategy

The experiment uses 6 months of production event logs (weeks 1–26), comprising timestamped page views, searches, clicks, add-to-cart actions, dwell times, and purchases across ~4M sessions. Each session is defined as a user's continuous activity within a 30-minute inactivity window.

The label is binary: a session is positive (1) if the user makes a purchase within 7 days of session end; negative (0) otherwise. This window captures both next-session intent and same-day repeat behavior, aligning with the business goal of identifying high-intent sessions for retargeting and ranking interventions.

### 3. Data Splits and Temporal Ordering

The data is split into three strictly chronological windows:
- **Training (weeks 1–16):** ~2.5M sessions
- **Validation (weeks 17–22):** ~800k sessions
- **Test (weeks 23–26):** ~800k sessions

Temporal splits are essential here for two reasons: (1) the hypothesis explicitly requires evaluation on "future weeks," and (2) random splits would mix weeks with different promotional calendars, seasonality, and traffic quality, confounding true model differences with distribution shift. The temporal boundary prevents forward leakage: a user may appear in both training and test, but their events and labels are chronologically disjoint, eliminating any use of future behavior to predict the past.

### 4. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Two parallel feature sets are constructed:

**Sequence model features:** Raw event sequences from the prior session, including event type (one-hot encoded: view, search, click, add-to-cart), product category, dwell time per event, timestamp features (hour, day-of-week), and device type. Sequences are capped at 200 events with zero-padding; longer sequences are truncated from the oldest end to preserve recency.

**Baseline model features:** Aggregated statistics computed from the same prior session, including event-type counts, total dwell time, time since last purchase, product category distribution (top 3 as binary flags), device and channel (categorical), and derived features such as search-to-click ratio and average events per minute. Both feature sets include global context: account age and historical conversion rate (computed from training data only) and label-independent promotional flags.

All preprocessing transformers (embeddings, scalers, imputers) are fit exclusively on the training set and applied without refitting to validation and test. This prevents any information leakage from future periods into the preprocessing step.

### 5. Model Training and Baseline

**Sequence model:** An LSTM or GRU-based recurrent network with 64–128 hidden units, dropout (0.2), and a sigmoid output layer for binary classification.

**Baseline:** XGBoost or LightGBM with max depth 5–8, learning rate 0.05, and 100–500 trees. Additionally, a second baseline variant is trained with explicit recency features (e.g., events in the last 10 and 30 minutes, exponential time decay) to control for the confound that the sequence model might be learning recency rather than true sequential structure.

Both models undergo equivalent hyperparameter tuning: grid search over 10–15 configurations on the validation set, optimizing the primary metric (see below). This ensures the comparison is fair and isolates the architectural advantage of sequence modeling from the effects of hyperparameter tuning effort.

### 6. Evaluation Strategy

**Primary metric:** Precision at 10% recall (P@R10). This metric directly reflects the operational use case: sessions are ranked by model confidence, the top 10% are selected for intervention (ranking boost, retargeting message, or promotion), and the fraction of those that convert is precision. This aligns with the business objective of catching high-intent users under a constrained action budget, avoiding false positives that waste intervention resources.

**Secondary metrics:** AUC-ROC (ranking quality across all thresholds), precision at 5% and 20% recall (sensitivity to budget constraints), Brier score and calibration curve (probability calibration), and log loss (overfitting check).

**Model selection:** The model with the highest validation P@R10 is selected. The test set is held out and accessed exactly once at the end to report final performance.

### 7. Controls for Confounds

**Recency confound:** The baseline is evaluated both with and without hand-crafted recency features. If the sequence model's advantage persists even when the baseline has explicit recency, it suggests the sequence model learns true sequential dependencies, not just recency.

**Traffic mix and promotion effects:** Test results are stratified by traffic channel (organic, paid, direct) and by promotion exposure to ensure the sequence model does not rely on channel-specific or promotion-driven signals. Weekly base-rate conversion is computed to flag any external disruptions.

All results are reported both overall and per-stratum, making confounds visible and quantifiable.

must_not_claim.claim: The inclusion of a strong baseline variant with explicit recency features may be seen as unfairly advantaging the baseline and invalidating the comparison.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: This is not a flaw; it is a necessary scientific control. The hypothesis is about sequence modeling's added value beyond what hand-crafted recency signals can achieve, not just modeling temporal data more richly. Including a stronger baseline strengthens the causal claim: if the sequence model still wins, its advantage is more meaningful. The addition of recency features in a baseline variant is essential to rule out the confound that the sequence model is merely learning recency patterns rather than true sequential dependencies.

must_not_claim.claim: Using the same user in both training and test sets introduces data leakage.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: User overlap across splits does not violate temporal integrity as long as the sessions themselves are temporally disjoint and chronological. Since labels and features are session-level and time-bounded, and no future data is used in training, there is no forward leakage. In e-commerce behavior modeling, users are active over months, so excluding them entirely from future periods is unrealistic and harmful to generalization. The critical point is the chronological boundary, which this design honors.

must_not_claim.claim: The primary metric Precision at 10% recall is insensitive to calibration and may reward overconfident models.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: This metric is appropriate because the downstream use case is ranking under a fixed budget, not probability estimation. High precision under fixed recall directly measures how many actual converters are captured in the top 10% of predictions. Calibration is monitored via secondary metrics (Brier score, calibration curves), but operational value lies in ranking performance, not probability fidelity. The choice is intentional and aligned with business impact.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer raises no objection, or raises concerns that are valid but already addressed in the design, allowing the defender to rebut them using controls explicitly described.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The design correctly handles all known confounds, respects temporal boundaries, avoids leakage, uses an operational metric, and includes a sufficiently strong baseline to isolate the value of sequential structure.

### 1. Objective and Hypothesis

Both models are evaluated on future traffic to ensure results reflect genuine predictive performance, not training-set artifacts.

This window captures both next-session intent and same-day repeat behavior, aligning with the business goal of identifying high-intent sessions for retargeting and ranking interventions.

### 3. Data Splits and Temporal Ordering

The data is split into three strictly chronological windows:
- **Training (weeks 1–16):** ~2.5M sessions
- **Validation (weeks 17–22):** ~800k sessions
- **Test (weeks 23–26):** ~800k sessions

Temporal splits are essential here for two reasons: (1) the hypothesis explicitly requires evaluation on "future weeks," and (2) random splits would mix weeks with different promotional calendars, seasonality, and traffic quality, confounding true model differences with distribution shift. The temporal boundary prevents forward leakage: a user may appear in both training and test, but their events and labels are chronologically disjoint, eliminating any use of future behavior to predict the past.

### 4. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Two parallel feature sets are constructed:

**Sequence model features:** Raw event sequences from the prior session, including event type (one-hot encoded: view, search, click, add-to-cart), product category, dwell time per event, timestamp features (hour, day-of-week), and device type. Sequences are capped at 200 events with zero-padding; longer sequences are truncated from the oldest end to preserve recency.

**Baseline model features:** Aggregated statistics computed from the same prior session, including event-type counts, total dwell time, time since last purchase, product category distribution (top 3 as binary flags), device and channel (categorical), and derived features such as search-to-click ratio and average events per minute. Both feature sets include global context: account age and historical conversion rate (computed from training data only) and label-independent promotional flags.

All preprocessing transformers (embeddings, scalers, imputers) are fit exclusively on the training set and applied without refitting to validation and test. This prevents any information leakage from future periods into the preprocessing step.

### 5. Model Training and Baseline

**Sequence model:** An LSTM or GRU-based recurrent network with 64–128 hidden units, dropout (0.2), and a sigmoid output layer for binary classification.

**Baseline:** XGBoost or LightGBM with max depth 5–8, learning rate 0.05, and 100–500 trees. Additionally, a second baseline variant is trained with explicit recency features (e.g., events in the last 10 and 30 minutes, exponential time decay) to control for the confound that the sequence model might be learning recency rather than true sequential structure.

Both models undergo equivalent hyperparameter tuning: grid search over 10–15 configurations on the validation set, optimizing the primary metric (see below). This ensures the comparison is fair and isolates the architectural advantage of sequence modeling from the effects of hyperparameter tuning effort.

### 6. Evaluation Strategy

**Primary metric:** Precision at 10% recall (P@R10). This metric directly reflects the operational use case: sessions are ranked by model confidence, the top 10% are selected for intervention (ranking boost, retargeting message, or promotion), and the fraction of those that convert is precision. This aligns with the business objective of catching high-intent users under a constrained action budget, avoiding false positives that waste intervention resources.

**Secondary metrics:** AUC-ROC (ranking quality across all thresholds), precision at 5% and 20% recall (sensitivity to budget constraints), Brier score and calibration curve (probability calibration), and log loss (overfitting check).

**Model selection:** The model with the highest validation P@R10 is selected. The test set is held out and accessed exactly once at the end to report final performance.

### 7. Controls for Confounds

**Recency confound:** The baseline is evaluated both with and without hand-crafted recency features. If the sequence model's advantage persists even when the baseline has explicit recency, it suggests the sequence model learns true sequential dependencies, not just recency.

**Traffic mix and promotion effects:** Test results are stratified by traffic channel (organic, paid, direct) and by promotion exposure to ensure the sequence model does not rely on channel-specific or promotion-driven signals. Weekly base-rate conversion is computed to flag any external disruptions.

All results are reported both overall and per-stratum, making confounds visible and quantifiable.
difficulty_justification: This case is moderately difficult because the soundness of the design depends on correctly weighing methodological rigor against operational realism. The absence of corruptions requires the reviewer to avoid over-criticizing scientifically sound choices that address real e-commerce-specific challenges, such as temporal leakage and recency confounds.

hypothesis: For credit card fraud detection, a sequence model trained on raw transaction event histories will detect fraudulent activity with higher precision at the same recall than a gradient-boosted classifier trained on aggregated per-card behavioral features.
domain: Finance — consumer card fraud detection for a large issuer processing continuous card-present and card-not-present transactions
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Sequence vs. Aggregated Models for Credit Card Fraud Detection

### 1. Data Collection and Label Definition

We would use 24 months of production transaction logs from the card issuer, encompassing approximately 500M–1B transactions (card-present and card-not-present combined). Each transaction record contains: card ID, transaction amount, merchant category code (MCC), timestamp, geolocation, device/channel type, and settlement status.

Labels are derived from chargeback disputes resolved within 120 days of transaction. A transaction is marked positive (fraudulent) if a dispute outcome confirms unauthorized use; processing errors, merchant disputes, and customer chargebacks are excluded. To avoid label censoring, we exclude all transactions from the final 120 days of the dataset, ensuring every transaction has had time to generate a label if one exists.

Expected fraud prevalence: 0.1–0.3% (highly imbalanced). This imbalance is realistic and will be addressed through class weighting in both models.

### 2. Temporal and Entity-Based Split Strategy

Fraud patterns drift over time—merchant fraud, organized fraud rings, and detection evasion strategies evolve. A random split would allow future transactions from the same card to leak into training, inflating performance estimates. We therefore use a **temporal, card-stratified split**:

- **Training set:** Months 1–12 (all transactions), stratified to exclude any card that appears in validation or test
- **Validation set:** Months 13–18 (all new cards not in training), further split into validation-A (months 13–15) for threshold tuning and validation-B (months 16–18) for confirmation
- **Test set:** Months 19–24 (all new cards not in training or validation), held completely until final evaluation

This stratification by card ID prevents the model from learning card-specific patterns that do not generalize. It forces both models to generalize to new customers, the realistic scenario at deployment.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

**For the sequence model:** We construct transaction sequences for each card, ordered by timestamp, containing up to 500 prior transactions (95th percentile of history in training set). Each transaction is encoded as: amount (normalized), MCC (categorical, one-hot or embedding), time delta since previous transaction (log-scaled), geolocation (coarse region, categorical), device type (categorical), and transaction type (card-present/card-not-present). Sequences are zero-padded to length 500.

**For the baseline gradient-boosted model:** We engineer 30 aggregated features over a 30-day rolling window per card: transaction count, mean/std/min/max amount, MCC diversity (entropy), geolocation diversity, time since last transaction, fraction of card-not-present transactions, device switching rate, merchant repeat rate, and others. Both models share the same 30-day lookback to ensure fair temporal context.

**Preprocessing:** All scaling, encoding, and imputation transformers are fit on the training set only and applied to validation and test without re-fitting. This prevents validation/test statistics from influencing the model.

### 4. Model Selection and Baseline

**Sequence Model:** Bidirectional LSTM with 2 stacked layers (128 hidden units each), temporal attention mechanism, and sigmoid output. Regularization: dropout (0.3), L2 decay (1e-5). Hyperparameters (learning rate, hidden size, dropout, attention heads) are tuned via grid search on validation-A, using precision at 90% recall as the selection criterion.

**Baseline:** Gradient-boosted classifier (XGBoost or LightGBM) trained on the same aggregated features. Hyperparameters (learning rate, tree depth, number of estimators, L1/L2 regularization) are tuned with equivalent effort on validation-A using the same metric (precision at 90% recall). Both models use identical class weighting to reflect fraud vs. legitimate transaction costs. This ensures the baseline is a fair, well-tuned competitor.

### 5. Evaluation Metric and Justification

**Primary metric: Precision at 90% recall.** In fraud detection, the business constraint is a fixed recall target (compliance requires catching at least 90% of fraud). Given that recall is fixed, precision determines the false-positive rate—the number of legitimate transactions incorrectly declined. Lower false-positive rate means fewer customer declines, better customer experience, and higher revenue retention. Precision at fixed recall directly measures operational value.

**Secondary metrics:** Precision-recall curve, recall at 99% precision, cost-sensitive loss, and per-card entity-level precision to verify no card-level leakage.

### 6. Model Selection and Validation Protocol

Hyperparameter tuning occurs on validation-A (months 13–15). Model selection is confirmed on validation-B (months 16–18) to prevent overfitting to the validation set. The test set (months 19–24) is untouched until final reporting.

### 7. Confound Controls

- **Entity stratification:** Card ID-based split prevents confounding from repeated-customer effects or card identity.
- **Recency ablation:** Compare sequence model to a degenerate sequence using only the 10 most recent transactions; if performance is equivalent, gains come from recency, not sequential reasoning.
- **Feature parity:** Both models receive identical temporal lookback, feature encoding, and class weighting.
- **Temporal drift:** Report precision at 90% recall per month of the test set to confirm stability across time.
- **Merchant category analysis:** Stratified precision by MCC to detect category-specific gains.

**Scope of claims:** The experiment establishes whether sequence models outperform aggregated boosting on precision at fixed recall *for this issuer's transaction distribution over the test period*. It does not establish general superiority across all fraud detection settings or isolate which aspects of sequence modeling drive the gain.

must_not_claim.claim: The temporal split uses unequal validation and test set durations (6 months each), which risks under-evaluating model stability and may not reflect real-world deployment cycles.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The 6-month validation and 6-month test sets are of equal duration and sufficient length to capture seasonal fraud patterns and ensure statistically robust estimates. The design intentionally reserves a full 6-month window for validation to support thorough hyperparameter tuning and threshold selection, which is justified given the complexity of the sequence model and the high stakes of recall-constrained operation.

must_not_claim.claim: Using precision at 90% recall as the primary metric ignores overall model performance and could favor over-conservative models that miss many frauds below the operating threshold.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Precision at 90% recall is operationally grounded: stakeholders must meet a minimum recall target for compliance. Within that constraint, maximizing precision (minimizing false positives) directly improves customer experience and revenue. The design includes secondary metrics like PR curves and cost-sensitive loss to evaluate broader trade-offs, making this a responsible choice for a business-driven evaluation.

must_not_claim.claim: The sequence model uses zero-padding to a fixed length of 500 transactions, which could introduce noise or bias for cards with shorter histories.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Zero-padding to the 95th percentile sequence length (500) is a standard and appropriate preprocessing step in sequence modeling with variable-length inputs. The model’s attention mechanism and masking are expected to handle padding correctly, and this approach ensures efficient batching without discarding valuable long sequences or distorting short ones.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic raises a concern, but the defender correctly rebuts it using domain and methodological reasoning specific to fraud detection, temporal evaluation, and operational constraints. The final verdict is that the experiment design is sound and supports a valid comparison.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The defender correctly explains that the temporal, card-stratified split prevents leakage, the metric aligns with stakeholder needs, and confound controls are sufficient given the scope of the hypothesis. Any raised issues are shown to be non-problems due to design choices being appropriate for the domain.

difficulty_justification: This case is moderately difficult because the design is methodologically sound and contains no corruptions, requiring the reviewer to correctly defend legitimate choices under potential scrutiny. The challenge lies in distinguishing between apparent red flags (e.g., fixed-length padding, temporal splits, recall-constrained metrics) and actually appropriate decisions grounded in domain-specific operational constraints.

hypothesis: A transformer model fine-tuned on raw radiology report text will predict 30-day readmission risk for hospitalized pneumonia patients more accurately than a baseline using only structured EHR features, when both are evaluated on future admissions from the same health system.
domain: Hospital readmission prediction from radiology reports and structured EHR data in acute-care hospital records
task_prompt:
To test whether transformer-derived radiology text improves 30-day pneumonia readmission prediction over structured EHR features alone, the following experiment is proposed.

**1. Data Selection and Cohort Definition**

We would use 3–4 years of retrospective inpatient admissions from a single health system, restricted to pneumonia diagnoses (ICD-10: J12–J18). The cohort includes all pneumonia admissions with available discharge disposition and 30-day follow-up data; no exclusions are applied to minimize selection bias. We expect 15,000–20,000 admissions, yielding 2,000–5,000 readmissions (typical 15–25% rate). Data sources: the electronic health record (structured fields) and the radiology information system (free-text chest imaging reports). The outcome is defined as any unplanned encounter (ED visit, inpatient admission, observation stay) within 30 calendar days of discharge; planned procedures and inter-facility transfers are excluded. Outcome data are obtained via linked encounter records and validated against claims data if available.

**2. Stratified Cohort Split**
