## Experiment Design: Raw Sensor Sequences vs. Hand-Engineered Features for Equipment Failure Prediction

### 1. Objective
To test whether a sequence model trained on raw multivariate machine telemetry and time-series dynamics predicts next-week equipment failure with higher precision-recall performance than a model trained on hand-engineered statistical summaries, while controlling for temporal leakage, site-specific overfitting, and maintenance intervention confounds.

### 2. Data Source and Preparation
The experiment uses 18–36 months of production data from 3–5 geographically distributed factories. Data sources include:
- High-frequency sensor streams: temperature, vibration, pressure, current, cycle counts, etc. (typically 1–5 minute resolution).
- Discrete state codes from PLCs and production controllers.
- Work-order and maintenance logs (timestamps, type, reason).
- Fault and alarm triggers from machine diagnostic systems.

Failure labels are defined as binary events: 1 if the machine experienced unplanned downtime, triggered a maintenance work order, or recorded an operator-reported fault within 7 days following the observation timestamp; 0 otherwise. Planned maintenance events are excluded. Any machine undergoing maintenance is held out from the prediction window during and for 14 days post-intervention to prevent the model from learning post-maintenance patterns unavailable in deployment. All data are verified for temporal consistency and sensor availability logged explicitly.

### 3. Train-Validation-Test Splitting Strategy
A three-layer split strategy prevents both temporal leakage and distribution shift confounds:

**Layer 1 — Temporal Split (primary anti-leakage measure):**
- Training: Months 1–12.
- Validation: Months 13–15.
- Test (temporal): Months 16–18.

This chronological ordering ensures that no signal from months 16–18 enters the training window. Strict timestamp audits verify no label information from future periods contaminates training features.

**Layer 2 — Stratification by Site and Machine Type:**
Within each temporal fold, data are stratified by plant and equipment class to ensure each split observes similar failure rate distributions. This prevents imbalance concentration in one fold and ensures both models see representative training conditions.

**Layer 3 — Holdout Site:**
One full production facility (or 20% of machines per site) is held entirely out of training and validation and reserved for final evaluation. This test set (test-holdout-site) measures generalization to genuinely unseen operating environments and isolates whether improvements are driven by site-specific nuisance patterns or robust failure dynamics.

### 4. Feature Engineering
Two parallel feature sets are constructed to enable a fair, controlled comparison:

**Sequence Features (Experimental):**
For each machine, extract all available multivariate sensor readings over a 7-day lookback window prior to the prediction date. Stack readings into a 2D time-series matrix: (time steps × sensors). Handle variable-length sequences with zero-padding and masking for missing sensors. Compute first-order differences (Δ sensor per 1h, 6h, 24h) to encode rate-of-change signals. Do not aggregate or summarize; feed raw temporal dynamics to the model.

**Summary Statistics Features (Baseline):**
For the same 7-day window, compute per-machine aggregations: min, max, mean, variance, skewness, kurtosis of each sensor; count of alarm events; days since last maintenance; maintenance event frequency; machine age; one-hot encoded machine type and plant. These are standard engineering features used in legacy predictive maintenance systems.

**Preprocessing:**
All preprocessing transformers (imputation, scaling, encoding) are fit exclusively on the training set (months 1–12). Missing sensor values are forward-filled up to 6 hours (typical sensor blackout duration); longer gaps use mean imputation from the prior 7 days of that machine. Validation and test sets are transformed using training-fit parameters. This prevents information leakage from future periods or holdout sites into preprocessing logic.

### 5. Model and Baseline

**Sequence Model (Experimental):**
A bidirectional LSTM with 2 stacked layers (128 units each), dropout (0.3), followed by a dense sigmoid output layer. Trained end-to-end on raw multivariate sequences with binary cross-entropy loss. Class weights are applied to handle imbalance (positive weight = n_negative / n_positive, capped at 10).

**Baseline Model:**
Logistic regression trained on the hand-engineered summary statistics. This is a fair comparison because it uses the same label, splits, preprocessing pipeline, and receives equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort. L2 regularization strength and class weights are tuned via grid search on the validation set.

### 6. Hyperparameter Tuning and Model Selection
For both models, a grid search is conducted on the validation set (months 13–15) over:
- LSTM: layer size, dropout, learning rate, lookback window (3, 7, 14 days).
- Logistic Regression: regularization strength (C), class weight.

Model selection is based on **Precision-Recall AUC (PR-AUC)** on validation data. The winning configuration is locked in and evaluated exactly once on the test set. No re-tuning on test data is permitted.

### 7. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary Metric — Precision-Recall AUC:**
PR-AUC is computed on the temporal test set (months 16–18) at the machine-week level (aggregating multiple daily predictions to a single label per week). PR-AUC directly reflects operational goals: it rewards high precision (few false alarms overwhelming technicians) while maintaining recall (catching real failures early). In imbalanced failure scenarios, PR-AUC is more informative than ROC-AUC.

**Secondary Metrics:**
- F1-score at a threshold calibrated to operational constraints (e.g., top 10% of alerts).
- Recall at 95% precision: true positive rate when false alarm rate is tightly controlled.
- Per-machine-type and per-plant PR-AUC breakdown: detects site- or equipment-specific confounds.
- Calibration curve: confirms predicted probabilities are trustworthy for scheduling decisions.

### 8. Confound and Leakage Control

**Temporal Leakage:**
Chronological split with explicit timestamp audits ensures no future information enters training. Feature engineering pipelines (imputation, scaling) are fit on training only.

**Site-Specific Overfitting:**
Holdout-site test set isolates performance on genuinely unseen plants. If sequence model underperforms baseline on holdout-site while outperforming on temporal test, the improvement is driven by site-specific artifacts, not robust failure dynamics.

**Maintenance Intervention Confounding:**
Machines undergoing maintenance are flagged and excluded from the 7-day prediction window during and after maintenance, preventing the model from learning post-intervention patterns unavailable in live deployment.

**Missingness as a Nuisance Pattern:**
Imputation rules are identical across models. Separately report performance on high-availability and low-availability sensor subsets to detect whether sequence models are exploiting missing-data signals.

**Statistical Significance:**
Confidence intervals (95%) on PR-AUC are estimated via stratified bootstrap resampling. Differences are considered substantial only if confidence intervals do not overlap.

### 9. Interpretation and Scope
This design establishes whether sequence models outperform summary statistics on **future, held-out temporal data and unseen production sites**. A positive result would support using raw telemetry and sequence learning. A null or negative result on the holdout-site set would indicate that improvements are overfitting artifacts rather than learning robust failure dynamics. The experiment does not establish causality; it measures predictive accuracy under operational constraints.
difficulty_justification: This case is challenging because temporal leakage is subtle and masked by seemingly robust stratification, while the metric mismatch requires understanding of operational constraints beyond statistical convention. A reviewer must reason about time-series validity and use-case alignment rather than pattern-matching standard practices.

hypothesis: For hospital-based claims analytics, a longitudinal model that uses the prior 12 months of diagnosis, procedure, and medication claims to predict 6-month progression to advanced chronic kidney disease will outperform a static baseline using only the most recent claim snapshot in AUROC and calibration.
domain: Healthcare claims analytics for chronic kidney disease progression risk in a payer or value-based care setting

## Experiment Design: Longitudinal CKD Progression Risk Model

### 1. Objective and Scope

This experiment tests whether a longitudinal claims model—using 12 months of diagnosis, procedure, and medication history—outperforms a static snapshot baseline in predicting 6-month progression to advanced chronic kidney disease. Success is defined as higher AUROC and better calibration on a held-out test cohort. The deployment context is early identification for proactive care management and nephrology referral in a payer or value-based care setting.

### 2. Data and Population

We use adjudicated medical and pharmacy claims from a single payer or health system warehouse spanning January 2020 to December 2022. The target population is adults aged 18+ with continuous enrollment for at least 18 months during the study period. Index dates are assigned uniformly across 2021, allowing 12 months of historical claims (lookback) and 12 months of follow-up for label construction. Members who disenroll before the 6-month post-index window closes are excluded from the analysis to avoid informative censoring bias; their count and baseline characteristics are reported separately.

### 3. Label Definition

The binary outcome—CKD progression—is defined as the occurrence of any of the following within 6 months after the index date: (a) diagnosis code escalation from early stage (1–3) to advanced stage (4–5); (b) initiation of dialysis, renal replacement therapy, or transplant; (c) first nephrology encounter or CKD-specific procedure. This definition emphasizes progression signals that are most reliably and promptly captured in claims, reducing ambiguity from repeated low-specificity coding patterns and aligning the outcome with actions that would trigger care-management escalation. Members are labeled negative only if they remain enrolled for the full 6 months with no progression signals.

### 4. Temporal Split Strategy

Data are split temporally on index dates into three consecutive, non-overlapping windows: (1) Training cohort: index dates Jan 1–Jun 30, 2021 (~40%); (2) Validation cohort: Jul 1–Sep 30, 2021 (~30%); (3) Test cohort: Oct 1–Dec 31, 2021 (~30%). 

**Justification:** CKD progression prediction is inherently temporal. Claims patterns, coding practices, and clinical guidelines evolve, creating temporal drift that a random split would leak into training. A temporal split prevents the model from learning future trends and simulates true prospective deployment: the model trains on historical members and is evaluated on genuine future cohorts. Because each index date cohort has a distinct 6-month follow-up window that does not overlap with prior or subsequent cohorts' index dates, cross-cohort leakage is eliminated.

### 5. Feature Engineering

All features are constructed from the 12-month lookback window (index date minus 12 months to index date) at the member level. No post-index information is used. Features include:

- **Diagnosis**: count of distinct CKD stages, count of comorbidities (diabetes, hypertension, CVD), presence of albuminuria, days since first CKD diagnosis
- **Procedures**: count of renal labs and imaging, count of nephrology/cardiology visits, temporal spacing
- **Medications**: indicators for ACE inhibitors/ARBs, diuretics, count of antihypertensive classes, fill frequency
- **Utilization**: inpatient admissions, ED visits, outpatient encounters, enrollment continuity (% of months with ≥1 claim)
- **Temporal patterns**: days since most recent CKD claim, regularity of claims (inter-visit intervals), specialist visit gaps
- **Baseline stage**: categorical variable at index date

All preprocessing (imputation, scaling, one-hot encoding) is fit on the training set only and applied identically to validation and test sets. Missing values (e.g., no specialist visits) are imputed as zero. This prevents information leakage from future cohorts.

### 6. Model and Baseline

**Intervention Model:** Logistic regression with L2 regularization (C tuned on validation set via grid search over [0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 100]). Logistic regression is chosen for interpretability, calibration properties (critical for probability-based outreach), and clinical transparency.

**Baseline Model:** Logistic regression using only the most recent pre-index claim snapshot, with features: CKD stage from latest claim, diabetes/hypertension indicators, age, sex, and enrollment duration. The baseline receives identical hyperparameter tuning effort on the validation set. This reflects current standard practice and is a fair null hypothesis.

### 7. Evaluation Strategy

**Model Selection:** Hyperparameter C is selected to maximize AUROC on the 30% validation cohort. The test set remains locked until final evaluation.

**Primary Metric:** AUROC—operationally aligned with risk ranking for prioritized outreach and capacity-constrained care management. It is robust to class imbalance and threshold-independent.

**Secondary Metrics:** 
- Expected Calibration Error (ECE): ensures predicted probabilities are trustworthy for budget planning
- Sensitivity at 90% specificity: operationally meaningful—catch rate when targeting top 10% of members
- Precision-recall AUC: secondary ranking metric
- Brier score: overall probability accuracy

All metrics are reported on the held-out test set only, with 95% confidence intervals from bootstrap resampling (1000 iterations).

### 8. Confound and Bias Controls

**Utilization Bias:** Test results are stratified by enrollment continuity (tertiles of % months with claims) and total claim count. AUROC and calibration curves are reported separately for each stratum. Substantial performance degradation in low-utilization groups would indicate confounding. Sensitivity analysis uses utilization-adjusted features (e.g., diagnosis diversity per encounter).

**Censoring:** Members lost to disenrollment are excluded, not assumed negative. Censoring prevalence and baseline characteristics are reported and compared across train/val/test cohorts to verify random distribution.

**Label Noise:** For a subset of members with linked laboratory data (creatinine, eGFR), we compute the PPV and sensitivity of the claims-based label against lab-confirmed stage change, contextualizing the performance ceiling.

### 9. Success Criteria

The hypothesis is supported if the longitudinal model achieves: (1) AUROC > baseline AUROC + 0.03 (clinically meaningful improvement); (2) ECE within ±0.05 on the test set; (3) stratified AUROC remains elevated even in low-utilization strata, suggesting true CKD signal rather than utilization capture.

planted_issues.description: The label definition no longer includes 'sustained stage escalation'—a key clinical signal that does not rely on procedural interventions or nephrology visits. As a result, the outcome is now narrowly tied to care escalation events (e.g., dialysis, specialist referral), which may not capture all true cases of physiological CKD progression. This shifts the target from predicting disease worsening to predicting utilization patterns, weakening the validity of the hypothesis test about longitudinal clinical signal utility.

must_not_claim.claim: The test set spans only the last quarter of 2021 and is smaller than the training set, suggesting inadequate evaluation power or potential bias.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The temporal split is intentional and methodologically sound: it simulates prospective deployment and accounts for temporal drift in claims behavior. The 30% test allocation is standard, and the index date assignment ensures sufficient follow-up time for label construction within the available data window. The design explicitly avoids leakage and maintains temporal consistency.

must_not_claim.claim: Excluding members who disenroll before the 6-month follow-up introduces selection bias and invalidates generalizability.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Excluding censored individuals is the correct approach here to avoid informative censoring bias. Labeling them as negative would be incorrect (they may have progressed after disenrollment), and including them without confirmed outcomes would dilute the label signal. The design appropriately reports censoring rates and characteristics to assess representativeness.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies that the outcome definition no longer captures 'sustained stage escalation' and argues this narrows the construct from biological progression to care escalation—a shift that undermines alignment with the hypothesis. The defender cannot plausibly justify this omission as an improvement, because it reduces sensitivity to true disease progression unaccompanied by procedural events.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer explains that dropping sustained stage escalation excludes patients who progress clinically but do not trigger procedural flags, thereby misaligning the label with the intended construct of 'CKD progression'. They reference clinical guidelines that recognize stage change via coding patterns as valid evidence.

difficulty_justification: The flaw is subtle because the corrupted label still includes high-specificity progression signals and appears clinically meaningful, but it systematically excludes non-utilizing progressors. A strong evaluator must recognize that the hypothesis is about disease progression, not care escalation, and that dropping sustained stage escalation breaks construct validity.

hypothesis: A sequence model trained on per-shipment event timelines and route-level context will predict late delivery risk more accurately than a tabular model using only aggregated shipment features for last-mile logistics operations.
domain: Transportation / logistics — last-mile parcel delivery delay risk prediction for a carrier or delivery platform
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Sequence Models vs. Tabular Features for Late Delivery Risk Prediction

### 1. Data Source and Label Definition

The experiment uses 24 months of production shipment records (Jan 2022–Dec 2023) from a single logistics carrier, spanning all cities, seasons, and operational conditions. Each record contains per-shipment event logs with precise timestamps (pickup, scan, sort, dispatch, delivery attempt, final delivery or exception), route attributes (distance, stop sequence, hub assignments), vehicle type, weather at delivery time, and historical driver/hub performance metrics. The label is binary: late delivery = 1 if actual delivery exceeds the promised delivery window; on-time = 0 otherwise. Labels are ground truth from operational records. Shipments that never reach final status are tracked separately and excluded from the primary analysis or flagged as a sensitivity check.

### 2. Temporal Split Strategy

The dataset is split chronologically, not randomly. Training set: months 1–12. Validation set: months 13–16. Test set: months 17–24. This temporal split is mandatory because (a) the hypothesis involves learning temporal patterns in event sequences; a random split would leak future information into training; (b) late delivery risk depends on real-time operational state, so a model trained on mixed-time data cannot be fairly evaluated on a held-out random sample; (c) in production, the model will predict on future data, so evaluation must respect this causal structure. The 8-month test window captures seasonal drift and operational changes that a shorter window would miss. All preprocessing and model selection use only training and validation data; the test set is never touched until final evaluation.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Two feature sets are constructed:

**Set A (Sequence-rich, for the sequence model):** Per-shipment event sequence (event types, timestamps, inter-scan gaps), route stop sequence (ordinal position, cumulative distance), temporal features (time-of-day, day-of-week, proximity to holidays), and hub/route context (hub processing statistics from training history, route-level late rate in prior week).

**Set B (Aggregated tabular, for the baseline):** Vehicle type, total distance, stop count, hub, weather, driver experience, driver on-time rate (prior 30 days), route-level late rate (prior 7 days), time-of-day, day-of-week.

No feature includes post-delivery information or ground truth labels. All transformers (standardization, one-hot encoding, missing value imputation) are fit on the training set only and applied to validation and test without refitting. Numeric features are z-score normalized using training statistics. Categorical variables are one-hot encoded; any new categories in val/test are mapped to 'other'. Missing values are imputed using training set medians for cross-sectional features and forward/backward fill for time series, but any attribute missing in >20% of training records is flagged and retained (not dropped).

### 4. Models and Baseline

**Primary model:** LSTM or Transformer encoder processing variable-length event sequences, concatenated with route context via an MLP head, outputting binary classification logits. This model is designed to learn temporal dependencies in scan patterns and processing dynamics.

**Baseline:** Logistic regression on Feature Set B. Logistic regression is transparent and receives equivalent tuning effort (regularization strength C and class weight optimized on validation data via grid search). The baseline does not receive Feature Set A because those are inherently sequence-based; the comparison tests whether sequence modeling outperforms a simpler model with access to domain context.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Area under the Precision-Recall Curve (PR-AUC). Late delivery is a minority class; PR-AUC is more sensitive to class imbalance than ROC-AUC and directly reflects the stakeholder's goal: identifying true at-risk shipments (precision) while capturing the majority of actual late deliveries (recall).

**Secondary metrics:**
- F1 score at validation-optimal threshold
- Recall at 90% precision (RP90): what fraction of late deliveries can be caught while maintaining acceptable false alarm rate
- Calibration metrics (Expected Calibration Error, Brier score): important because model outputs may trigger costly interventions
- PR-AUC, recall, and F1 stratified by city, season, route type, and hub to detect whether gains generalize or are concentrated in specific subgroups

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameters are tuned on the validation set (months 13–16) using grid search, with PR-AUC as the selection metric. The model (sequence or baseline) achieving the highest validation PR-AUC is then evaluated exactly once on the test set. No threshold adjustment, feature engineering, or re-tuning occurs after test evaluation.

### 7. Confound Controls

To isolate the effect of sequence modeling: (1) Both models receive comparable domain features (route context, weather, hub identity) to control for information access disparity. (2) Temporal leakage is prevented by chronological splitting. (3) Recency bias is checked via ablation: if the sequence model's advantage is driven primarily by recent event signals rather than temporal dependencies, the hypothesis is qualified. (4) Subgroup stratification reveals whether gains are uniform or concentrated in specific conditions. (5) Missing data handling is analyzed separately to ensure the sequence model does not gain advantage through inadvertent missingness strategies. (6) The baseline's hand-crafted historical aggregates are computed using only past data available at prediction time, preventing lookahead bias.

### 8. Claims and Scope

This design establishes whether a sequence model outperforms a tabular baseline on held-out future data, controlling for temporal dependence, class imbalance, and known confounds. It does not establish causality of specific temporal patterns or interpretability of learned features; that requires additional analysis (feature attribution, ablation studies).

must_not_claim.claim: The temporal split is invalid because the training and test sets have different class distributions due to seasonality.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Seasonal variation in late delivery rates is a real-world phenomenon the model must handle. The temporal split intentionally preserves this realism and evaluates generalization to future conditions. A random split would artificially inflate performance by smoothing over drift and invalidate the causal prediction setup — the model will face changing class distributions in production.

must_not_claim.claim: Using PR-AUC instead of ROC-AUC is inappropriate because ROC-AUC is the standard metric for binary classification.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: PR-AUC is preferred for highly imbalanced problems like late delivery prediction (where positives are 5–15%) because it focuses on performance in the high-precision region and is more sensitive to differences among good models. ROC-AUC can be overly optimistic under imbalance and misalign with operational goals of minimizing false alarms while catching true late shipments.

must_not_claim.claim: The baseline model should be allowed to use aggregates derived from event sequences (e.g., mean scan interval) to make the comparison fair.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The hypothesis tests whether modeling the full temporal event sequence provides value beyond static tabular features. Giving the baseline hand-crafted summaries of sequence content would conflate the benefit of richer input data with the benefit of sequence modeling. The current design isolates the value of temporal dynamics by keeping raw sequence information exclusive to the sequence model.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer acknowledges that the design is methodologically sound and raises no valid objections. The final verdict is that the experiment supports a fair evaluation of the hypothesis.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The design adheres to temporal split best practices, controls for confounds, uses a stakeholder-aligned metric, and ensures fair model comparison by preventing leakage and inappropriate data advantages.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer raises a concern, but the defender successfully rebuts it with domain- and hypothesis-specific reasoning (e.g., justifying PR-AUC for imbalance, defending the chronological split, or explaining baseline feature restrictions).
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The concern stems from a general ML heuristic but does not apply given the real-world causal constraints and operational goals of last-mile delivery prediction.
